So this is crucial p5 plus this is crucial's first entrance into the gen 4 nvme drives if you haven't known crucial is owned by micron and micron produces chips for every nvme drive out there so your drive might have microchips but let's see what this one offers so p5 plus comes in capacities 500 gigabytes one terabyte and two terabytes and it's priced relatively low for a gen 4 drive it's around 100 us dollars for 500 gigabytes model 180 us dollars for one terabyte like i have here and 360 us dollars for 2 terabyte model so in terms of its performance it's rated to deliver sequential speeds of up to 6.6 gigabytes for read and 5 gigabits per second for right and you can manage upwards of 720k and 700k random read write iops fan task to full load this is thanks to dynamic write acceleration and this is crucial's own slc caching implementation the p5 plus supports stream smart data reporting secure erase capability as well as hardware accelerated aes 256 bit full disk encryption based on the tcg opal 2.0 specification so when enabled it can keep your data locked down and save from the hand of thieves if you are to ever lose your precious hardware to unforeseen circumstances because it is opel 2.0 compliant it's also windows bitlocker compliant too this is an extremely handy feature to have if you have a specific need for this so one thing surprised me when i received this package here it is so this is the first drive that comes with its own screw so no more finding the box of the motherboard just to find that one screw so this is a nice little detail it might not mean much to some but again this is a nice gesture it features a completely black pcb and a black sticker over the top this drive is not to be used without additional heatsinks just fyi as it can get pretty hot during heavy workloads the controller used in its is crucial's own in-house design so no fyson here it's an eight-channel design that leverages lp ddr4 dram to accelerate fdl management the 500 gigabyte model and one terabyte model utilize one gigabyte of dram while the two terabyte has two gigabits of dram it's also sports nvme autonomous power state transition aps-t for short and adaptive thermal protection that kicks in at a temperature of 80 degrees celsius and hotter to protect your data you also get integrated power loss immunity the main performance improvement with this drive over the competition is the nand latest micron 176 l tlc chips actually on this model we have two of them each 512 gigabytes exact number is b47r 176 ltlc and this is one of the fastest nand on the market today when compared to the previous generation 96 ltlc the new one is 30 shrinked in size it also changed in the way it functions resulting in greatly reduced cell-to-cell capacitive coupling issues and also it allows for increased program pulse ramping all this provides not only lower read and write latencies and boosted throughput but also improved reliability and endurance the endurance here is rated at 300 terabytes written for the 500 gigabytes model 600 terabytes written for the one terabyte model and 1200 terabytes written for the two terabyte model still there are drives with much higher endurance but this will last you more than a warranty period is which is five years crucial storage executive lets you monitor the drive as well as update its firmware and enable certain features such as momentum cache which is similar to samsung's magician's rapid mode dram caching the company also provides downloads for acronis true image for crucial letting you clone data from all drives to the p5 plus but enough of me rambling about the stuff on and on so let's see the benchmarks [Music] do [Music] so judging by the performance i can say this drive is not the fastest one out there but the emphasis here was not on the speed it's more on the features it has that may be more beneficial than the pure speed even though speeds are pretty fine for a gen 4 drive features like hardware accelerated aes 256-bit encryption are nice to have also in my opinion this is a nice all-around nvme to have endurance rating is more than okay speeds are way above what most gen 4 drives can accomplish and it comes with a 5 year warranty what can be improved are these sustained speeds maybe this could be done through the future firmware updates but given its competitive price i would say that this is one nicely balanced nvme drive thanks for watching guys if you like the content please subscribe to the channel like and share the video and also thanks crucial for sending the drive for the review see you in the next one [Music] you

[Music] the crucial p5 1 terabyte m.2 ssd is known for being one of if not the first ever drive that is based on microns dm01b2 controller this controller has an eight channel design and also has full dram support the memory chips are made by micron and have a 96 layer tlc9 design but more on those later on the p5 is available in several capacities and several prices starting with 250 gigabytes priced at 55 usd or euros 500 gigabytes priced at 80 us dollars or euros one terabyte at around 150 us dollars and finally two terabytes at 330 usd or euros and all these variants have a manufacturer's warranty of five years we start with the way the ssd looks because even for this product it matters especially for those that want to make the best looking system out there the crucial p5 uses the standard m.2280 form factor which means that the ssd itself has a length of 80 millimeters and a width of 22 millimeters and if you didn't know this before well now you do in addition the design of the crucial p5 is stylish with an all-black color scheme that will match any system regardless of what other components you have installed the reason why i'm talking about this is that a lot of manufacturers will just slap any pcb color usually the cheapest they can get their hands on and any paper level this ruins the whole aesthetic of the system especially if your drives are into the view the memory chips used on this drive are made by micron and have the 96 layer tlc nand design each chip has a total storage capacity of 512 gigabytes for a total of 1000 gigabytes or one terabyte of storage capacity the controller used on the p5 is the micron dm01b2 this is the highlight of this m.2 drive as it is a brand new controller from micron it has an 8 channel flash design and full dram support in addition the dm01b2 also has dual arm cortex r5 cpu cores the microcontroller interfaces with the host over a pcie 3.0 x4 interface and links to the nvme 1.3 protocol speaking of which the crucial p5 has a single ddr4 4266 ram chip which also uses a total of one gigabyte of fast storage installing the ssd is fairly easy but perhaps someone is watching this and is not confident in their knowledge regarding the installation of an m.2 ssd first of all you get the ssd locate the m.2 socket on your motherboard and insert the ssd with the correct orientation you must do this with the ssd at a slight angle and then lower it into the slot finally the rear side of the m.2 ssd is secured to the motherboard with the help of a small metallic screw which you should already have given that your motherboard comes with a couple of them and now we can move on with the testing of the crucial p5 one terabyte ssd i am going to start the testing with a real world test which is copying of files from one folder inside the crucial p5 to a different folder on the same drive the use files have a total combined size of 100 gigabytes and in this test you can clearly see that the speed remains steady at around 1.26 gigabytes per second however this is with a relatively small chunk of data when compared with the total drive capacity in the next test we can see that the crucial p5 can easily write around 210 gigabytes of data before the speed drops thanks to the cache being filled up this is not bad at all in addition the cache recovery speed of the p5 is very good actually this ssd recovers its cache almost immediately after the data was written this is possible because crucial uses an adaptive cache which expands when large files are used on the drive the third test involves the now well-known crystal dismark benchmark now reaching version 8.And in this test the p5 1 terabyte reached an average speed of around three thousand five hundred megabytes per second for the reads and three thousand two hundred megabytes per second for the rights which is quite close to the advertised specifications the next test is the ato disk benchmark which will give us a rough idea of how the performance is for the tested ssd in addition having pure numbers is great for comparing the p5 with other ssds and storage devices and in this test the crucial p5 one terabyte sits very close to the samsung 970 evo 500 gigabytes which is a great thing to see especially since that drive is just a little bit faster and the final test is the loading of a level within the shadow of the tomb rider video game the game itself is running at 1080p with all the graphical settings turned up to their maximum value direct x12 mode is enabled and pure hair is disabled and in this test the crucial p5 needed approximately 20 seconds to load the first level of the game a value which plays it on the second position in the chart being behind the samsung 970 evo and also on the same level as the crucial p1 however there are issues for some reason the crucial p5 runs warmer than expected with the new controller which does use dual arm cortex r5 cpu cores the crucial p5 reached around 100 degrees celsius mind you this was without a heat sink applied on the ssd with a heatsink installed the maximum temperature dropped to around 70 degrees celsius which is acceptable the way the thermal testing is done is pretty simple just copy massive file over a longer period of time from one drive to the reviewed ssd and watch how everything gets very war the crucial p1 one terabyte ssd is a good nvme drive that has a good performance for its price point speaking of which the crucial p5 in the one terabyte variant is available for around 150 usd or euros which is not that expensive but it's not exactly a bargain either especially when other models on the market can be had for less and match the performance of the p5 the best thing about the crucial p5 is the real world performance while synthetic benchmarks are really nice to look at the real world performance is where everyone has their interest in the drive achieved on average a file transfer speed of 1.26 gigabytes per second which is great especially since modern video games are increasing in sizes and so does the size needed for our storage devices the crucial p5 uses a pseudo slc type cache which in the case of this drive has a size of around 93 gigabytes the good thing about the size of the cache is that it is large enough to dampen any right or read burst of speed and thus allow the ssd to deliver a smooth speed and performance in addition this size should be enough for most workloads in your daily usage given that the software and games that will write more than 100 gigabytes of data at the same time are far in between however once the space limit is reached then the speed of the drive will lower until the file transfer or writing activity is completed afterwards the ssd will recover its cache and it's back to the usual performance one issue with the crucial p5 is the thermals with no heatsink installed and tested for a longer period of time the p5 easily reached a temperature of 100 degrees celsius which will trigger the ssd to thermal throttle and lower its temperature and thus lower its performance however once a heatsink was installed over the ssd the temperature dropped to around 70 degrees celsius at maximum load which is much better fortunately most if not all modern motherboards do have their own m.2 heatsinks included so this temperature issue shouldn't be present but it is a good thing to be aware of it if you liked this review then you can perhaps consider subscribing for more and also if you want to support the channel in a direct way then in the description below you can find both the patreon and subscriber star pages of the channel

Every single time I post an SSD video I get tons and tons of comments telling me that I'm completely missing out on the SK hyx drives and then especially so dp41 platinum and that I should definitely about how this p41 Platinum performs how it compares to some of the best performing high-end drives that you can currently get and most of all should you buy this drive or not let's begin the p41 is a Gen 4 nvme SSD and you can get it in three different capacities 500 gbes 1 TB and 2 TBT and I have the 2 tbte version right here there are no heat sync versions available but as you will see later in this video putting it under a heat SN is definitely recommended but the most interesting part about this drive is that SK HX uses their own airis controller and their own 176 layer 3D TLC nand which is something that very few Brands can do this drive also comes with a 2 GB Dam cache as well as a large SLC cache feature so on paper it does look like a proper high-end drive it also comes with a 5-year long warranty and a total bytes written rating of 1200 tabt for the 2 Tab model which is pretty standard in this high-end segment both AES 256 and TCG pyite encryptions are supported explicitly which is not really relevant for a lot of consumers but if it matters to you it is something that most other drives do not mention at all but let's dive straight into the performance numbers and just like in every other SSD video I'm going to start with the PC Mark 10 quick Benchmark now this is a very nice bundle of different tests that simulate all those simple little things we do with rpcs every single day so working with various documents for example looking at your vacation photos loading your games and so on and this is a very useful Benchmark to look at if you're looking for a secondary drive or an extra drive that you want to use for those simple little things and as you can see the p41 Platinum started out really well the Samsung 990 Pro and The crucial t500 are a little bit faster as are the brand new Gen 5 drives but the p41 Platinum is faster than all other Gen 4 ssds in the list including some popular options like the SN 850x the KC 3000 the fury Renegade and so on that being said you shouldn't really buy these very expensive high-end drives for this very light use case so let's move on to the full PC Mark 10 Suite which is a test that simulates a bit more serious a bit more intense and definitely more constant use of the drive and this is a great Benchmark to look at if you're looking for a new main drive or if you need to run some applications that can be very heavy on your SSD like in my case U editing videos for example and I would say that this is a much more important and more relevant comparison if you're looking to buy an expensive high-end drive and again the p41 Platinum does really well compared to the other Gen 4 drives it sits right next to the 990 Pro and it only has the t500 ahead of it while all other Gen 4 ssds are anywhere from slightly to pretty far behind looking at latency we got more or less the same result it does really well it is just behind the crucial t500 and it is ahead of most other Gen 4 drives including the 990 Pro the consistency test is not that relevant for a lot of you because it simulates a very extreme and very long multi-hour workload that most of you will probably never ever do but it is still very good to see how a drive holds up when you really stress it for a really long period of time and then especially so when it comes to expensive high-end drives and here the p41 Platinum did drop a few spots but it still looks completely fine it is right next to the fauda 530 it is ahead of the KC 3000 and the WD black SN 850x it is behind The crucial t500 and the fury Renegade and it game folders around recording your gaml and so on and obviously this is a very useful Benchmark if you're looking to buy an SSD that you will mainly use for gaming and here the p41 Platinum performs really well the t500 is a bit ahead once again but it is the second fastest Gen 4 SSD in the list beating the SN 850x which was the fastest gaming drive until recently and if we just look at the gaming results that I personally find most important like loading times installing times and updating times it ended up scoring about 94% of the t500 which is the fastest Gen 4 drive that I've tested So today i'm going to talk about the sabran rocket 4 plus which is a gen 4 and mimi ssd that is supposed to offer a lot of performance for a reasonable price it is also one of the very rare options let's see how it performs and how it holds up when compared to about 20 other gen 4 ssds that i've tested so far let's go 2 2280 model so it fits the majority of desktops laptops and consoles but it won't fit in your steam deck they're using the fyson e18 controller that is found in the majority of gen 4 ssds and they're combining it with a dram cache and some tlc flash chips which is again a typical way to put together an ssd there's not that much in terms of features technically disc control supports hardware encryption but sabrin doesn't seem to mention it anywhere so if that is really important to you i would go for a brand that openly supports it it also has an endurance rating of 700 terabytes written per terabyte capacity and it comes with a five year long warranty if you register your drive so again this is all pretty typical i would say that the most interesting thing about this ssd are the capacity options so you can choose between a 500 gig one terabyte two terabyte four terabyte as well as eight terabyte models which is very unique but let's talk performance so the pc mark 10 quick benchmark contains various tests that replicate all those little light tasks that we do with our pcs on a daily basis so those are the things like working with documents working with photos for example but also loading your games so this is a very good benchmark for anyone that wants to add a second or an extra ssd to their systems for those very simple tasks and the rocket 4 plus looks pretty good here taking a spot close to the top of the chart the s 770 is the odd one out since it doesn't use dram and it actually benefits greatly from a high-end ddr5 test rig that i used to test all my ssds but it's not far behind some other high-end drives like the sn850 kc3000 and corsair mp600 pro it is also ahead of the fire cuda 530 and the samsung 980 pro as well as its predecessor the rocket 4.Now another drive to keep an eye on here is the crucial p5 plus which is just ahead in this test and is generally one of the cheapest gen 4 drives on the market now the full pc mark 10 suite is a more intense test that is meant to replicate more serious and more active use of your system and of the drive itself so this is a very good test if you plan to use this drive as your main drive or if you plan to run some applications that are very heavy on the ssd and here the sabrin drops to roughly the middle of the graph but it's still an upgrade over the older rocket four now the entire top half is actually pretty competitive with the s 770 and the kc 3000 standing out but the 10 or so drives behind them are all pretty close together the pc mark 10 consistency test is usually where cheaper drives start to struggle because this is a very extreme very long stress test that pushes a drive to its complete limits so it's not that relevant for most of you but it's still a great test to see how an ssd performs when really stressed for a really long period of time and here the rocket 4 plus goes up a few spaces again landing right behind the crucial p5 plus and the wd black sn850 while still being an upgrade over the old rocket 4 drive and this is a result of sabrin sticking to tlc memory instead of costing down to qlc because qlc drives are usually found at the bottom of this graph still i wouldn't really call this the most logical ssd choice for a workstation setup but it's not a bad result at all pure sequential speeds are not something that i personally believe are that important most brands love them for marketing purposes but they're not that relevant for an actual day-to-day performance even when you're looking for an ssd for your playstation for example things like access time and latency are way more important than the actual straight up speed still sony does have a minimum read speed requirement i personally think that it's not necessary to focus on it that much but if you are looking for an ssd for your playstation i do In this video, I'm going to talk about the new Corsair MP700 Pro XD SSD.It is the first Gen 5 drive with a Fisen E28 controller to appear on the market.And that alone should be a big step forward in terms of performance.So let's see how it actually performs, how it compares to other drives I tested so far, and let's see if it's worth getting it or not.Let's begin.[music] The MP700 Pro XD is available in three capacities at the moment.One terabyte, two terabytes, and four terabytes.With the 8 terabyte option coming to the market in early 2026, they don't come with any heat sinks this time around, but as you will see later in this video, you will definitely want to add some cooling to these drives.If you look at the spec sheet, it is actually pretty generic.They don't really specify which components are being used, just that it is 3D TLC memory.And all the performance metrics are actually mentioned with the up to value, so they don't really mean anything.It is a Gen 5 drive, of course.The total bytes written rating of 700 terabytes per terabyte capacity is about what you would expect to see in this higherend segment.And you also get the expected 5-year long [music] warranty.But let's dig a bit deeper into the hardware side of this.Starting with the new Fisonson E28 controller, which is the biggest feature of these new Gen 5 drives.A lot of SSDs on the market are based on Fison controllers, but this is the first retail drive to use the latest E28 one, which promises to offer better performance than the previous E26 models, as well as the Silicon Motion SM2508 base [music] drives.Next to the controller, we do have some DRAM cache as well as Kioxia BICS8 3D TLC flash module.So on paper, this is a proper high-end drive.But let's see how this drive actually performs.And as always, I'm going to start with the PC Mark 10 quick benchmark, which is a collection of tests that simulate a lot of those simple little things we do with our PCs every single day.So things like working with your documents, looking at your vacation photos, loading your games and so on.And this is a very useful benchmark for anyone that is looking for a drive to use for those simple little tasks.So it is definitely not a use case that requires an expensive high-end Gen 5 SSD like this one, but you still want it to perform well in this test.And the MP700 Pro XT easily took the first spot in the graph with a pretty big gap behind it.It is significantly faster than the older E26-based SSDs, but also the WD Black SN8100 and the Fury Renegade G5.Full PC Mark 10 suite simulates a more serious and a more intense use of the drive.And this is a great benchmark to look at if you're looking for a new main drive or you need to run some applications that can be a bit heavier on your SSD.And here the Corser took the very first spot yet again with a pretty significant margin.The SN8100 is a bit closer, but the MP700 Pro XT is far ahead, which clearly shows the impact that a brand new controller can make.If we look at latency, the drives are more or less in the same order with this new Corsair being nicely in the lead.The consistency test is not that relevant for most people because it simulates a very intense and a very extreme multi-hour long workload that most of you will never ever do.But it is always interesting to see how a drive will hold up when you stress it so much for such a long time.And then especially so when it comes to very expensive high-end Gen 5 drives like this one.But in this test, the MP700 Pro XD dropped down quite a bit, scoring only a 623 mgabyte per second average with some of the fastest drives in this test scoring double that or even more.Now, this is the only benchmark that is a bit more sensitive to the capacity of the drive tested.But even if we limit the results to two TBTE models, there are drives that are clearly much faster in this test.[music] 3D Mark Storage is a benchmark that simulates a lot of different gaming related tasks like [music] loading games, installing your games, recording your gaming, moving game folders around and so on.And this is a very useful benchmark for anyone that is looking for an SSD to use primarily for gaming.In this test, the MP700 Pro XT took the third spot, landing just behind the SN8100 and the T710.But all three of them are very very [music] close.And if we just look at the gaming results that I personally find most important, which is loading times, installing games, and updating your games, it comes [music] in second.But again, the SN8100, the T710, and this MP700 Pro XD are very close together.Sequential read and write performance numbers don't really represent proper real life use as well as previous benchmarks do, but it can still be a useful metric for some people.Now, we've seen many SSDs that hit the limits of a gen 5 slot.So, if we look at sequential rights, drives that are at the top of this graph are all very close together.The two SM2508 drives do score slightly higher here, but that really doesn't make that much of a difference.[music] When it comes to sequential reads, it is a big tie at the top as well as they're all limited by the slot.This also means that this drive is well above Sony's required read speed for a PlayStation 5 use.But as I said many times before, PlayStation does not support PCIe Gen 5 speed.So, buying an expensive Gen 5 drive for a PlayStation will not really make any sense at all, and you're way better off buying a more mainstream and less expensive DRM based Gen 4 SSD instead.[music] One of the upsides of this new, E28 controller is its reduced power consumption.So E26 drives, they usually use up to about 10 watts, while these new E28 models should sit closer to 5 to six watts during heavy workloads.But even a couple of watts of power on such a small surface means that the drive can get hot and you do want to add some form of cooling to it, especially if it sits under your GPU.So, you should either use the heat sink that comes with your motherboard or you can buy a simple third-party heat sink for a couple of euros or dollars on Amazon.[music] U that should be more than fine.If you don't know which one to get, I'm going to leave a couple of suggestions in the description box down below and you can just go ahead and check those out.But let's talk about what you have to set aside for a brand new E28based drive like this one, which is a very tricky topic because all brand new SSDs always launch with a price that makes absolutely no sense before it quickly goes down and then settles on a more sensible spot.And it's even harder to compare it to other drives during the holiday season when a lot of older products are on a very good sale.But let's try to make some sense out of all this.If I look at the 4 TBTE MP700 Pro XT right now, it is available in one shop here in the Netherlands with a price tag of €570, [music] which is technically a little bit under its MSRP.But when you consider that you can get a 9100 Pro for around €200 less, it makes no sense to go for the Corsair.The situation in the US is a bit better with the MP700 Pro XT costing $460.So, it is cheaper than the Fury Renegade G5 and only slightly more than the SN8100, but it is still almost $100 more than the 9100 Pro.Interestingly enough, the gap between Gen 5 SSDs and Gen 4 SSDs is actually shrinking a little bit.So, it is slowly becoming a little bit more reasonable to pick up a Gen 5 model compared to an older Gen 4 one.Anyway, [music] a new controller or not, for this Corsair to make any sense at all, the price will need to come down quite a [music] bit.And if it does, I think this new MP700 Pro XT and E28based SSDs in general could be very attractive.They're extremely fast in real world trace benchmarks.Their top tier SSDs for gaming.And while they do lack performance in the extreme consistency benchmark, that will mostly make a difference in more niche extremely intense 24/7 workloads.So, if you're looking for a proper high-end SSD in the future, you should definitely keep an eye on this one, but only when the price goes down.For now, it is just way too expensive.[music] Anyway, that is all I had for today, but before I go, let's check out the sponsor of this video.These new mid-range power supplies are pretty affordable, but they still come with all the features you would expect to see on a proper high-end power supply.They are fully modular.They are very efficient and very quiet due to their fan design.Plus, they come with a hybrid fan mode that using the links in the description below.Thank you all for watching and sticking to the end, hopefully it was interesting enough.But if you liked it and you want to see more content like this one, please do consider clicking that subscribe button so you never miss my future uploads.[music] Bye guys, and I will see you all in the next one.Bye.>> [music] >> for you.Hey

In this video I'm going to talk about corsair's brand new SSD the mp700 elite now this is the first drive that uses fison latest Gen 5 controller and it is also the first proper Gen 5 SSD to drop the DRM cache and use host memory buffer instead now dless drives are still quite controversial for a lot of you but keep in mind that a lot of hmb drives perform extremely well and most importantly it lowers the cost of the drive so this P 700 Elite should become one of the most affordable Gen 5 options on the market so let's see how it performs how it compares to other ssds I've tested so far and design they went with this time around even though in most builds you probably won't be able to see it at all now the heat sink is removable and underneath is a pretty simple single-sided drive with a controller and two flash chips and as I mentioned at the start it has Faison's new E31 controller that is supposed to be more affordable and more efficient than the older e26 controller that was used for pretty much all Gen 5 ssds until now if we look at corsair's own website they don't actually list the exact controller and memory they're using just that it is 3D TLC nand and while I do looking for a drive that you mostly want to use for these simple little things and the mp700 elite had an average of 716 mbes per second which is more in line with the better Gen 4 ssds than the previous Gen 5 ones so it's not a bad score but seeing how well hmb Gen 4 drives performed I expected a bit more in this test now the full PC Mark 10 Suite simulates a bit more intense and a bit more serious use of the drive and this is a great Benchmark to look at if you're looking for a new main drive or you need to run some applications that can be very heavy on your SSD like editing videos for example and here the mp700 elite ended up where I expected it to be so in between the top Gen 4 ssds and the higher end Gen 5 ssds now ideally I would have liked it to be a bit closer to the mp700 pro se which sits at the top of the graph instead of closer to the Gen 4 t500 and 990 Pro but again not a bad score at all now looking at the latency it is pretty much in line with previous results filling the Gap yet again between earlier Gen 5 drives and the best Gen 4 options on the market the consistency test is not that relevant for a lot of you because it simulates a very extreme a very intense and a very long multi-hour workload that most of you will never ever do but I still believe that it is very interesting to see how a drive holds up when you're really stress it for such a long time and as expected the mp700 elite did drop here like any other drml drive but it also dropped a lot further than I expected it to as scoring below affordable hmb Gen 4 drive so there are definitely limits to what the new controller can do when you really stress it and if you plan to do any an intense workload this is not a great option to go for especially when you compare it to the 990 Pro for example or even cor's own mp700 Pro Se that sits at the top of the graph with the result that is almost seven times higher than this one now 3D Mark storage is another collection of various tests that simulate a lot of different gaming related tasks like loading games installing games moving game folders around recording your gaml and so on and of course this is a very useful Benchmark for anyone Gen 5 nvme ssds are slowly coming to the market and Corsair just released their very first gen 5 Drive the mp700 compared to gigabytes auras 10 000 that I've tested before this Corsair mp700 is supposed to be significantly cheaper and actually in stock which is already a great way to jump start a product so let's see how it performs how it compares to the gigabyte as well as bunch of other ssds and let's see if it's worth considering a Gen 5 SSD at all let's begin I have a two terabyte model right here but you can also get a one terabyte version as well there's no mention of other capacities and I think it is a shame not to have a four terabyte option as well because I'm pretty sure that the target audience for really fast ssds pretty much overlaps with the target audience for large capacity drives if we look at the specs we can see that it is built around Faison's New e26 Gen 5 SSD controller and micron's latest 232 layer 3D dtlc nand and of course it has some dram cache as well the one terabyte model gets two gigabytes of dram cache which is twice as much as you would usually find on a high-end Gen 4 drive and the two terabyte model comes with a four gigabyte cache so it has exactly the same specs as the gigabyte auras 10 000.That being said Corsair is a bit more open with their specs so they list sequential reads and writes of up to 10 000 megabytes per second which is the limit of this controller and some random performance numbers as well Hardware encryption is supported and you get a 1400 terabyte written warranty on this two terabyte model or a five year long warranty whichever comes first now realistically you are more likely to run out of the five year long warranty way before you write 1400 terabytes to a single Drive even though the power consumption is less than 10 watts which does create a lot of heat for a small SSD of course there is not including a heatsink but they do say that you should use your own like the one that came with your motherboard but I'll talk about thermals a bit later in this video let's first see how this drive performs and as always I'm going to start with the PC Mark 10 quick test this is a great Benchmark for anyone that wants to add a second SSD to their system and it is pretty much a bundle of different little tests that replicate all those simple light things we do with our PCS every single day things like looking at photos opening documents and so on the the mp700 does really well here with the same result as I got with the auras 10 000.They outperform even the fastest Gen 4 ssds by quite a bit however there is no reason to buy these high-end ssds for simple light use cases so let's move on to something more challenging like this full PC Mark 10 Suite now this is a test that replicates a much more intense more serious and more consistent use of the drive and I would say that this is a very useful Benchmark to look at if you're looking for a new main drive for example or for anyone that needs to run applications that can be heavy on the SSD like editing videos for example and here again the mp700 ended up with the same result as the aorus 10000 which puts both Gen 5 ssds ahead of Gen 4 Drives By about 30 percent if you look at the fastest 990 Pro and closer to 50 ahead of most other high-end Gen4 Drive lives but between the two Gen 5 models there seems to be no difference at all which also makes sense because they use the exact same Parts but let's check the PC Mark consistency test now this is an extremely heavy Benchmark that stresses the drive non-stop for a very long time so it's not really relevant for a lot of people but it is still interesting to see how a drive behaves under such an extreme and multi-hour long workload that just pushes the drive to its very limits and here the mp700 falls behind with a score of only 433 megabytes per second which puts it somewhere in the a tier of Gen 4 ssds but far behind the 990 Pro as well as the mp600 pro lpx it is also far behind the aorus 10000 which it should be able to match at the very least so it does feel like they should check this out that they should tweak it a bit and probably fix it with a firmware update but when it comes to gaming the situation is actually the other way around in the 3dmark storage test which is a combination of benchmarks that include a lot of gaming related tasks the aorus was disappointing but the mp700 takes the crown beating the sn850x by about 30 percent and the Gen 5 auris by about 37 if we look at the gaming results that I think are the most important like loading times and installation times and update times it ended up scoring about 125 percent of the fastest Gen 4 Drive I've tested so far which is the sn850x as I always say sequential read and write numbers are not that relevant from a practical perspective as they don't really represent the real world use of a drive but it is still very important to check if a drive can reach the speeds that are mentioned in its spec sheet and here the mp700 ended up very close to the aorus 10 000 intern terms of Rights and it matched it exactly in read speeds so the output is definitely a lot higher than with Gen 4 drives but since the theoretical limits of a Gen 5 SSD should be twice as high as Gen 4 I do expect even better numbers with future Gen 5 drives now obviously any SSD that can transfer 10 gigabytes in a single second needs some form of cooling but as I said at the start Corsair does not include a heatsink now they originally planned to launch this drive with a heatsink that had a teeny tiny fan but luckily they dropped that idea because even a tiny fan on an SSD could be very noisy and very annoying to a lot of people that value silent builds so they decided to just go without one but you absolutely do need a heatsink because without it the drive can begin to throttle in a matter of minutes so you can use the heatsink on your motherboard or you can go for a simple third-party one because even with a simple Heating and a little bit of airflow the drive was kept under 60 degrees Celsius during a stress test so for a realistic workload a In today's video I'm going to talk about Kingston's latest high-end Gen 4 nvme SSD the fury Renegade it is pretty much their top tier drive that is supposed to compete with the highest performing drives on the market like the 990 Pro the sn850x and even Kingston's own KC 3000 which is really interesting because on paper the KC 3000 and this Fury Renegade have the exact same Parts but the Renegade has been tweaked a bit to make it perform even better which definitely explains why this was one of the most requested SSD reviews in the past couple of months so let's begin even though this is a high-end Drive Kingston ships it in the same basic package they use for their budget and V2 drive now less waste is definitely better but I do prefer having a package that you can open without completely damaging it because if you decide to do so it might be harder to return it after it has been opened you can get a version with or without a heatsink and as you can see I have the version without it that comes with a low profile graphene heat spreader instead it is available in capacities from 500 gigabytes all the way up to 4 terabytes and I really like that they included the four terabyte model as well lots of high-end ssds just stop at two terabytes which is really a shame I do have the two terabyte version here which is dual sided so do keep that in mind for specific use cases that require a single-sided SSD that being said Kingston does specify on their website that the 500 gigabyte and one terabyte models are single sided if you look at the specs we can see they use the Faison E18 controller which is a pretty common controller in a lot of high-end drives and that they're using a 3D TLC nand memory although they don't specify which one this particular model seems to be using 176 layer Micron nand which is the same as I had on my KC 3000 and that one is actually among the best options out there the specs also don't mention a dram cache but Renegade does come with a 2 gigabyte ddr4 cache just like the KC 3000 but one thing that is different from the KC 3000 is the endurance rating while it seemingly uses the exact same Parts the fury is supposed to be a bit more durable as you can see by its higher total bytes written rating now in my opinion this is mostly a theoretical Advantage because in reality no one will hit that number before the five year long warranty expires so I wouldn't really put too much value into that also any SSD can break anyways so you should always have backups no matter what but let's talk about performance and how it compares to the rest of the high-end drives I've tested so far and as always I'm going to begin with the PC Mark 10 quick Benchmark which is just a collection of different little tests that are meant to simulate all those little light things we do with our PCS on a daily basis so things like working with various documents or looking at photos for example and as I always say this is a great Benchmark for anyone that wants to add some extra storage to their system for these simple little tasks and here the fury Renegade starts off pretty well it is an objectively good result and it is in line with some other Faison E18 bass drives like the mp600 pro lpx or other high-end Alternatives like the sn850x also it gave the same result as the Kingston KC 3000 now the full PC Mark 10 Suite is a more intense test that is supposed to replicate a more serious and more consistent use of your system and this is a great Benchmark to look at if you're looking for a new main drive or if you need to run some applications that can be heavy on the SSD like editing videos for example and here it sits nicely in the top part of the graph once again matching the KC 3000 and nicely competing with the WD sn850x it is a little bit behind the Samsung 980 Pro as well as the New Gen 5 aurus 10000 but those drives cost a lot more looking at the latency result you get the same picture it is definitely one of the faster Gen 4 drives out there even though the PC Mark consistency test is not really relevant for most of you I do think it is interesting to see how a drive behaves under an extreme workload that lasts for hours and completely stresses the drive to its very limits and surprisingly enough the fury Renegade pulls away from the KC 3000 with a score of 673 megabytes per second average compared to the 343 on the KC 3000 I even retested the kc3000 again to make sure I wasn't missing anything but the results ended up exactly the same now it is really hard to say what exactly is causing this difference but considering they have the exact same controller same nand and similar dram cache it just feels like Kingston has improved the firmware for the fury Renegade but not for the KC 3000 either way now it does compete well with the mp600 pro XT and looks really good compared to other high-end drives like the sn850x or dp5 plus but let's look at gaming now 3dmark storage Benchmark is a bundle of tests that include a lot of game gaming related tasks like installing games recording games saving games loading games as well as moving game folders around and here it goes back to matching the KC 3000 near the top of the graph which is again completely fine if I just look at the gaming results that I personally find more important so loading games and installation and update times it all looks pretty similar it scored just under 90 percent of the performance of the fastest gaming Drive I've tested so far which is the sn850x now I don't think that sequential read and write performance is that relevant as it doesn't really represent a proper real-life use as well as the previous tests but on the other hand it is also interesting to see if a drive at least comes close to the speed they claim in their spec sheet now in sequential rights it scores just over 6 500 megabytes per second average which puts it yet again at the top together with the KC 3000 and a few other drives and only the Gen 5 SSD is far ahead as it isn't capped by the Gen 4 port in sequential reads it was just shy of 7000 megabytes per second which is also in line with other top tier Gen 4 drives that also means it is well above Sony's recommended spec for the eps5 which together with the gaming Benchmark means I would definitely consider this drive for PlayStation 5 use thermals seem a bit better than on the KC 3000 even without a heatsink but with a little bit of airflow it was very hard to get it to overheat and to throttle after a few hours of the heavy consistency test it still stayed under 80 degrees Celsius on the sensor and around 71 degrees on the flare camera which is a lot better than on most Gen 4 drives I still do think it is wise to put it under a heatsink especially if it's in a spot where it's not getting any air as I said at the start these drives can come with a heatsink as well but if that costs a lot lot more than this non-hitsync version it is better to just put it under the heatsink of your motherboard or get a third-party model that will cost you around ten dollars on Amazon links are in the description of this video as always so overall it does feel like a slightly improved KC 3000 it's not actually faster in most regular use cases and is definitely not much better as some people claim but it does come with noticeable changes that will allow it to handle those extremely heavy workloads and thermals much better so in my opinion it is definitely the better option between the two assuming the price makes sense if I look at the U.S prices it only costs a few dollars more than the KC 3000 if you're into those really heavy workloads it would be a few dollars well spent but for most regular users I do think it's a little bit expensive a two terabyte crucial P5 plus will cost you thirty dollars less and it will get you most of the performance as well here in the Netherlands the price are a bit more favorable it costs about the same as the P5 plus while being faster and it is a bit cheaper than the sn850x while generally performing at a similar level the KC 3000 is only slightly cheaper but looking at these prices the fury Renegade does make a lot of sense but as always prices do vary all the time they're different in different regions and you might be able to dig out some nice discounts so before you buy anything always do your homework and check all the prices in your region before making a decision because if the price is right this Fury Renegade is definitely a great drive to consider if you're after a high-end Gen 4 SSD this video was brought to you by Corsair and their Elite LCD XD coolers these Premium All-In-One water coolers combine excellent performance with a fully customizable LCD screen that can showcase anything you have in mind with a low noise pump and the AF Elite fans they can easily keep up with the latest and hottest of processors out there while keeping your system quiet you can install them on a variety of sockets including the latest Intel and AMD ones and once the installation is complete you can use the IQ software to control the RGB effect or sync them with the rest of your Corsair gear check them out using the links in the description below that is all I have for this video I really do hope you enjoyed it if you did consider pressing that subscribe button to never miss my future uploads bye guys and see the next one [Music]

Dual Lane standard of USB so you're going to be capped at maximum of 1,000 megabytes per second even with the T9 SSD but if you're buying an SSD you should also be considering future support so even if you're not able to use those dual Lane speeds now you may be able to in a future computer that you purchase both of these drives are pretty fast and testing on my 2021 MacBook Pro I was able to hit roughly the same speed on both the t7 shield and and the T9 most users aren't going to notice any difference between the performance of them they both also support Samsung's password protection T9 does not have a water resistance rating both of the drives include a USBC to USBC cable as well as a USBC to usba a cable and the drives have the same physical dimensions and they weigh about the same too both drives come in 1 2 and 4 TB configurations and the t7 shield has multiple colors that you can buy the T9 is only available in the dark gray black color so if you're trying to decide between the T9 and the t7 shield I think there's a couple quick things that you can think through first and foremost The t7 Shield is about half the price of the T9 if you're buying the 4 TB model then you're not even going to be able to take advantage of the faster speeds that the T9 offers so in that case you also should really consider just buying the t7 shield if you're not even going to be able to use the faster speeds that the T9 offers but at the same time it's also great to buy a drive that's going to last you a long time and if you choose to buy the T9 you're going to have that futureproof 3.2 Gen 2 2x two port on it just in case a future computer that you buy is able to take advantage of those speeds by the t7 Shield if you don't have the USB 3.2 2x2 ports and if you want a more affordable and more durable SSD by the T9 if you have a computer that supports the 2x2 USB ports or if you just want to be prepared for a future computer that's going to be able to take advantage Hi everyone ada here and today i'm going to talk about a brand new external ssd from samsung the t7 shield it is actually launching today and this time around samsung decided to fully focus on durability so this little drive is supposed to be waterproof it is supposed to be dust proof and it is even supposed to be drop proof so it should be a perfect option for anyone that takes their external ssd wherever they go or just simply use the ssd in situations where it might be damaged whatever those may be now the t7 series isn't really new it has been around for about two years now and while they are still the most sold external ssds pretty much everywhere in the world they were never the fastest drives you could buy so let's see how this one performs and if it's worth considering it all let's begin 2 gen 2 connection which is fast enough for most systems but it would have been nicer if they updated to a 20 gigabit connection instead now on the box itself it says it is compatible for pcs macs and mobile devices but honestly most ssds that i've tested so far work on most devices anyway so like any other option on the market this will work on a playstation xbox or on video cameras that support recording on an ssd so compatibility is not something that you should worry about when it comes to this drive but when it comes to hardware encryption a lot of manufacturers actually have decided to drop this feature completely and i really like that samsung continues to offer it so with their software you can actually easily password protect and encrypt your sensitive data now keep in mind if you constantly want to lock and unlock your data on different machines all the time i do think that the t7 touch with the fingerprint sensor is just much easier and quicker to use because you don't have to use the software but of course if you choose that one then you will lose the extra durability you will get with the t7 shield but let's look at that performance i'm going to start with the pc mark 10 data drive benchmark and this is actually a very nice and a very quick test that is meant to replicate a fairly light use of your drive so those are the things like working with documents photos and applications now the first t7 wasn't really known for its amazing performance and this new t7 shield doesn't seem to perform very different from the original now to be honest any ssd is going to do these tasks just fine so if you just open documents or edit some photos you cannot really tell the difference between most of these drives in the list right here but that is also not really an excuse for samsung not to show any improvement and not to get a bit higher up in this graph in the pc mark 10 suite which is a longer more intense test that combines a lot of real world scenarios into one score and that is meant to replicate a more active and a more heavy use of the drive the new t7 shield is a tiny bit ahead but not by that much it is not a big enough difference to really notice it in real world anyways and as you can see in the graph there are actually several other options out there that offer significantly faster performance if that is what you are after and we got a similar picture in the 3d mark gaming benchmark as well and this is a fairly new benchmark that combines loading times of several different games into one score as well as an average latency result and i haven't really had time to retest all the drives that i have but the result so far shows the same thing as before so the d7 shield isn't really any faster than the original t7 and there are faster options out there that will load your games a bit quicker than this one while sequential performance isn't that relevant when it comes to internal drives it can be pretty relevant when it comes to external ssds which are very often used to just copy data to and from now a drive with a 10 gigabit usb port will have read and write speeds roughly capped at that point and obviously 20 gigabit drives will show better performance but it is also important to remember that there are very few devices out there that actually offer a 20 gigabit port while 10 gigabit ports are actually quite common also grabbing a 10 gigabit over a 5 gigabit one makes more sense too because the price difference between the two is not that big nowadays so i would say 10 gigabit is pretty much a sweet spot a really nice thing about the t7 shield is that it can hold that right speed until the drive is completely full so there doesn't seem to be any thermal throttling at all or at least not in our indoor testing which is done at 23 degrees celsius many drives tend to throttle when you start riding hundreds of gigabytes without a break and then especially those super compact and light ones or some of those very fast ones so if you're using this in a scenario where you write a lot of data non-stop like for those big clunky video files the t7 shield will be a great choice so on one hand i do think it's disappointing that after almost two years samsung has done very little to improve the t7 performance and for most regular workflows the performance is unchanged and there are just plenty of faster drives out there that will probably cost you less now i don't really know the exact price of this one just yet but i do expect it to be a bit more than the t7 and right now many alternatives cost less than the regular t7 but on the other hand they're also putting a lot of time and effort into some parts of the drive that many many other manufacturers just ignore things like proper ip rating a hardware encryption and improved cooling to prevent that thermal throttling so this d7 shield is pretty much made for those specific cases so for anyone that really needs that extra durability or maybe for anyone that plans to use this as a camera recording ssd out in the field and so on and in those cases where price matters less and those extra features make sense this drive will be an excellent choice for anyone else i'm just going to go ahead and put some suggestions in the description down below based on the prices at the time of filming this video now that is all for today let me know in the comments down below if you have any questions or if there is any drives that you would like me to look at i'll do my best to read all of them thank you all for watching and see you in the next one bye guys [Music] you

This is the samsung portable ssd t7 shield external solid state drive it happens to be a follow-up to the portable ssd t7 touch the one with the fingerprint scanner on it for additional security benefits what makes it so different from the t7 touch and most other external drives is its durability you can see how the t7 touch is a bit thinner since it's just an aluminum frame but the t7 shield adds a layer of protection even though they share the same dimensions every year i read or hear about one of my fellow creators on how their external drive stopped working usually it's a freak traveling accident or from frying the insides due to spilling water all over it but what makes the t7 shield so special is that it can handle dust rain and falls without breaking but also offers great security features now samsung did send the t7 shield out for me to test out and was kind enough to sponsor this video but i've also convinced them to send me three extra one terabyte drives to give away so if you want one all you have to do is like the video be subscribed to the channel and follow me on instagram or tiktok make sure to let me know what color you want in the comments along with your username extra bonus points if you have a failed drive story now there's three different colors to choose from beige blue and black personally i'm a big fan of the blue color there's two storage sizes one terabyte and two terabytes inside the box is the drive itself a usb type c to type a cable a usb type c to type c cable and the installation guide it's a small drive and only weighs 98.5 grams and measures 88 millimeters wide by 59 millimeters tall and 13 millimeters thick the t7 shield is encased inside of an aluminum frame and is protected by thick wrap around rubber padding the padding also makes it very easy to grip and prevents it from sliding off any slippery surfaces there's a type c port to connect the cable and led indicator that lights up in blue every time the drive is being used the t7 shield is compatible with most devices i had no issues hooking it up to my macbook pro or my alienware x14 windows laptop it even works with android devices like my samsung galaxy z5 once connected the drive is detected and immediately there's even software that can be installed on mac or windows that allows you to encrypt the drive using 256-bit aes hardware-based encryption i also recommend installing it regardless of whether you plan to encrypt your drive because samsung is constantly pushing out firmware updates the drive was more than fast enough to edit my video footage i recorded both pro res and sony xavc using 42 to 10 bit footage 4k video editing only really needs around 400 megabytes a second read and write speeds but the samsung t7 shield more than doubles the speed with 1074 megabytes a second read and 1024 megabytes a second right to get even more nerdy samsung has moved from the fifth gen 92l 96t v-net found in the t7 touch to more efficient 6th gen 128l 136t v-net which should bring about a 10 improvement in latency and 15 reduction in power consumption also because i added multi-cam footage it needs to access two streams at once and the t7 shield did it flawlessly i copied a lot of files to and from my pc as well as to and from my mac with speeds being equal on both operating systems fun fact the drive even works with my xbox series x in case you need extra storage for video games this is especially true if you play call of duty warzone now i do recommend installing samsung's magician software because it has so many management tools to keep the t7 shield in tip top shape the drive dashboard gives you a nice overall look to see if the drive health is in good working order and even tells you the operating temperature which can be handy if you're working in a cold or hot climate the drive can be renamed and if you click on the performance benchmark it will test out the maximum read and write speeds just note that if you want the best speeds make sure the usb port you're connecting it to is usb 3.2 gen 2 compatible older gen usb ports don't have the same amount of bandwidth there shouldn't be an issue if you've bought a computer in the last few years now because the samsung t7 shield is a rugged drive i decided to put it through a few tests to see if it can withstand the elements the first test was a water test and since the t7 shield is ipx5 certified it can withstand water being sprayed at it for up to three minutes this past my water test without any issues after i dried it off i plugged it back into my computer and got the exact same read and write speeds as i did prior to getting it wet the second test was the dust test and because i don't have actual dust i decided to just pour baking soda all over it to give it a new white color i left it dirty for about 10 minutes and then washed it off with water i was going to keep the baking soda on but leaving white powder on your ssd is a good way to get arrested for drug trafficking just like the water test it got the exact same read and write speeds as before the last test was the drop test and dropping your drive is more likely to happen to most people it can be dropped up to 9.8 feet or 3 meters not only did i chuck this thing off my desk but i dropped it from 9.8 feet high and survived without any problems honestly i don't foresee any problems of this being in a bag and getting tossed around when traveling i even decided to throw it down the stairs to mimic falling out of a pocket and of course it survived that too i'm very impressed how durable it is i've been on a lot of planes with cramped overhead compartments and tiny spaces under the seat to put your back my luggage is constantly getting compressed hit and tossed around making a rugged drive like this a no brainer now if you're thinking of picking one up the one terabyte model retails for 98 usd but i suggest going for the two terabyte models especially if you edit videos like me as video footage tends to be quite big and takes up a lot more space the two terabyte samsung t7 shield retails for 199 us or 270 canadian for those of you that depend on storage for your work regardless of whether you're a video editor financial analyst or photographer don't cheap out on the drive honestly it's just not worth it i've read too many horror stories of malfunctioned external drives due to spilled liquids or freak travel accidents spend a few more dollars on a rugged drive like the samsung t7 shield seriously i'd be so angry if i had to redo a video especially when i'm on a deadline time is money let me know in the comments below if you've had an external drive fail on you maybe we'll start a ready community of failed drive stories at least we'll have a place to complain about it but in the meantime if you're interested in picking up a samsung portable ssd t7 shield there'll be links in the description down below also don't forget to follow and drop a comment share your failed drive story with your instagram or tick tiktok username if you want to win one of these sweet ssds feel free to like the video if you found it useful and to subscribe thank you for watching and see you guys in the next one

Apple charges $1,200 for a 4 tbte SSD upgrade on a MacBook but you can get the same storage for just over $300 with an external drive today I'll walk you through why you should consider buying an wanted to give you the short answer before getting into the details over the past 10 years I bought several Macs and I've never gone for more than 512 GB of internal storage instead I always use an external SSD saving myself a lot of money currently upgrading from the 512 GB to 4 terab when purchasing a MacBook Pro costs an outrageous $1,200 the only time I'd recommend upgrading the internal SSD is if you're constantly taking your MacBook in and out of a backpack several times a day even then it's hard to justify paying that much just for the convenience of not plugging in a USB device every time you need to access your files plus if your computer dies retrieving files from the internal SSD can be challenging on the other hand if your files are on external SSD transferring everything to a new computer is a breeze my first Samsung Drive was the T3 then I had two T5 and now I'm using a 4 tby t7 and a 2 tab T9 the reason why I stick with these drives is because they're reliable and affordable even my old T3 is still working perfectly after all these years and right now the 4 tbyt D7 on Amazon cost just over $300 which is a steal for what you get I primarily use it to store and edit my videos directly from the SSD or my MacBook Pro while the 2 TB T9 is permanently connected to my 24in iMac when it comes to differences between these two drives the main one is the speed Samsung claims the T9 can reach up to 2,000 megabytes per second while the t7 tops out at 150 mbes per second so nearly double the speed however and this is a big however if you are a Mac User you won't notice any difference in speeds between the T9 and the t7 as of today no Apple computer supports USB 3.2 2x two so the performance of these two drives will be virtually identical that's why I you'll rarely notice the difference between 1,000 megabytes and 4 or 5,000 megabytes per second maybe if you're copying a ton of files you might see the difference but with apple Now using apfs as their main file system most duplicated files are just references to the original for me when editing videos there's virtually no difference between using external or internal storage most of the time the drive's performance is limited by some other factor for example my T9 is always plugged into my iMac and stores all my recent video footage so after I finished shooting I dump the files onto the external SSD however even with the T9 reaching up to 1,000 mbes per second my camera's SD card is much slower so the transfer speed is bottlenecked by that in short it's tough to find scenario where you'd actually notice a speed difference if your drive is faster than let's say 600 megabytes per second to wrap things up these drives have been my goto Mac accessory for the past 10 years and I The Crucial X10 Pro and the Samsung T9 both offer really fast speeds up to 2100 megabytes a second, but they both also require the use of USB 3.2 Gen 2 dual lane capabilities in order to get these faster speeds.And in this video, we're going to compare both of these drives and discuss when you should choose to buy the Samsung and when you should choose to buy the Crucial.Now, I want to start this video off by saying Crucial did send me the X10 Pro for purposes of making some videos, but all the thoughts in this video are my own.They have no say in it.I did buy the Samsung T9 with my own money and I've been using both of these for a little bit now and have a couple thoughts on the differences between these two drives.Unfortunately, if you have a Mac, you're not going to be able to take advantage of the dual lane speeds.And doing some tests on my 14-inch Mac, the speeds came out to be very similar.Although, on the X10 Pro, I did seem to get a more consistent speed across both the read and write.The Samsung T9 does not have an IP rating, but it does have a drop rating of up to 9 ft.The Crucial X10 Pro has an IP rating of 55 and it has a drop rating of up to 7 1/2 ft.If you compare both the drives side by side, you'll notice that the Samsung T9 is definitely considerably bigger than the Crucial X10 Pro, but they both are still pretty compact when it comes to external SSDs.You can also buy both of these drives in one, two, and 4 TBTE configurations, and I've been testing the two TBTE versions of both.Samsung and Crucial also both give you a 5-year warranty on their up to about 800 to 950 megabytes per second which is still pretty fast and going to be great for most users but you're not going to be able to hit the top speeds of this drive if you're using a Mac I love fast external SSDs, but if you want to get top performance, you're either going to need a drive with USB 4 or Thunderbolt if you want to get top performance.So, today we're going to look at the this in my M4 Max with Thunderbolt 5, I was getting speeds all the way up to about 3,400 megabytes a second on both the read and the right.So, this will achieve the faster speeds if you use it with a faster Thunderbolt 5 computer than if you're just using it with Thunderbolt 4, but they're pretty close in terms of performance in the real world.The Extreme Pro with USB4 is designed to be very fast and also to work well even under heavy load and to continue to perform over prolonged use.Whenever I transferred 900 GB of files from this to my computer's SSD, it only took about 4 minutes.So, I was Hi guys this is Madi, in this video I’m goanna show you sandisk extreme pro ssd unboxing, do a speed test, take a look at its features and compare it with non-Pro version and finally, let’s see if this device is worth buying, I have sectioned this video for you so you can easily go to any section you, let’s start.Speed test: Sandisk’s theoretical maximum speed for this drive is 1050MB/s.These are in ideal lab conditions, so the real-world result of 950MB/s is still incredibly impressive, and well within what I would call acceptable margins from the quoted max speeds.The consistency of the drive was also impressive, I don’t think I have ever seen such consistency before.Both portable SSD V2’s are upgrades of their respective predecessors.To be more specific, the V2 variants offer twice the speed of their predecessor.For example, the SanDisk Extreme Portable SSD (V1) has a speed of up to 550MB/s.The Extreme Portable SSD V2, its successor, offers up to 1,000MB/s of transfer speed.And the Extreme PRO Portable SSD Version2 offers up to 2,000MB/s or 20Gbps of transfer speed.Its predecessor offers up to 1,000MB/s; similar to what the non-Pro Version 2 now offers.Both the Sandisk Extreme PRO Portable SSD V2 and non-Pro Portable SSD V2 are IP55 certified.This means that these drives have dust protection and water-resistant.It is not water-proof, but it can resist water from low-pressure jets from all directions.The Sandisk Extreme Pro SSD is an NVMe version of the Extreme Portable SSD, and Sandisk claims that they are capable of reaching speeds up to 1050 MB/s, compared to the 550MB/s of the slightly cheaper models.At first glance, the drives look identical, but the side-by-side comparison shows us that the new Pro drives are slightly larger than the non-Pro version.The Sandisk Extreme Pro SSD is a worthy addition to the brand’s lineup.With the newer NVMe technology, this drive is twice as fast as the slightly cheaper Extreme Portable SSD from their catalogue.Although the size of the drive has grown slightly to accommodate the size of an NVMe drive, it still remains the smallest on the market and will easily slip into a pocket.In fact, you could fit several of these in your pocket, let alone your camera bag.Thank you so much for watching this video, see you soon in the next video.

Hi today we're going to take a look at the sundays extreme pro portable ssd version 2.So let's see what it can do [Music] today we will be taking a look at the second generation of sandisk extreme pro main differences between this second generation drive and the first one and how it may or may not affect your buying decision while the first generation drive used the now very common usb 3.1 gen 2 which has a maximum transfer rate of 10 gigabit per second the second generation version doubles the speed to 20 gigabit per second using the usb implementers forum's super convoluted usb 3.2 gen 2 by 2 naming scheme so far this all sounds great but the problem is that at the time of writing this in mid 2021 there are almost no laptops if any and very few motherboards that natively support usb 3.2 20 gigabit per second if you have a desktop pc your best bet is to buy an expansion card which is exactly what we had to do since without it and even when connected to a thunderbolt 4 port on our brand new computer we're still getting only 10 gigabit per second transfer speeds the external design of the xtreme pro version 2 is identical to the first version the only way to tell them apart is the sandisk logo on the back from our perspective it would be nice if they had the big v2 markings somewhere as well the drive has the same semi porous rubberized coating as the previous generation and a single usbc connection it also has an ip55 rating with some protection against splashes and falls other than the usb upgrade the only thing that really changed with this drive has to do with the our usb type a connections on our new computer are operating way too slowly we are still investigating and we double checked our results on another fast computer just to be sure the one thing that always works is the subscribe button so hit it and hit the bell icon we're back and we're going to take a look at performance now we performed two types of tests a synthetic test using the crystal disk mark 8 software and a real world test where we transferred over 200 gigabytes of videos stills and other files to the drive and back with different cables and on different connections all these tests were done using our new pc and you can see the specs on the screen right now now let's take a look at the results using usb 3.1 or usb-c to usb-a cable with the sandix xtreme pro ssd version
