Hi i'm gordon from camera labs and this is my review of the sandisk extreme pro portable ssd version 2 a rugged pocket-sized usb drive that exploits the size speed and robustness of the latest solid-state storage it works with macs and pcs as well as compatible phones tablets and games consoles and is available in one or two terabyte sizes i tested the latest one terabyte version launched in september 2020 which costs around 230 dollars or pounds and claims speeds up to two thousand megabytes per second although the maximum performance will only be attainable on devices with the fastest usb ports as i'll explain later it's also really important to check the quoted speed of the drive when ordering one as sandisk has so far made several versions of its portable ssds rated between 550 and 2000 megabytes per second the slower models are of course cheaper and may be good enough for your needs the last one i tested was an extreme version one 250 gigabyte model 18 months earlier rated at 550 megabytes per second and that still feels pretty fast to me today so let's see how much faster the xtreme pro version 2 model is and crucially who will benefit from its faster speeds but before delving into the actual performance in real life why would you want a portable drive in the first place well they're invaluable for backing up your data transporting large files or collections or simply freeing up space on a full computer so you can complete a project i've relied on a small fleet of portable drives for years from providing backup i can take off site to actually hosting assets for large video projects portable ssds have become fast enough for you to actually edit video straight off them coming close to or even matching the speeds of internal drives i also find them invaluable for getting photos and videos off onto my phone or tablet while gamers may use them to expand their storage some high-end cinema cameras can also record directly to ssds although do check for specific compatibility first as they can be quite fussy older spinning hard disks still work out cheaper than ssds at the time i made this video you could buy a four terabyte western digital my passport drive for a little less than the cheapest one terabyte portable ssd or less than half the price of the one terabyte extreme pro version 2 drive i'm testing here while boasting 4 times the space of either hard disks aren't as fast nor as compact as a portable ssd and are also more vulnerable to knox but remain compelling value for money i still use them for offsite backups of my photo and work collections but i've now switched to ssds for tasks where speed and pocketability are more important okay so how to sand this latest drive measure up the extreme pro version 2 measures 110 by 57 mil that's just a bit longer than a credit card and is just over 10 millimeters thick that's similar in thickness to a modern phone but much smaller overall and at 84 grams much lighter too so it'll easily slip into almost any pocket much of what differentiates one portable drive from another is the case and finish and sandisk has once again gone with a rugged style sporting silicon on the rear for grip and rounded corners for comfort in your hands or pockets i really like it there's also a handy cut out allowing you to attach it to a keyring or hang off a carabineer or a d-ring most useful of all though is the ip55 rating for water and dust resistance before you get too excited though you can't submerge the drive in water and expect it to survive but it should shrug off mild splashes rain or moisture so long as you're careful not to expose the port it also offers reasonable protection against shock and vibration sandisk claims it'll survive a two meter drop and again while it's not invincible it will better protect your data from knocks and small falls than a mechanical hard disk like all modern portable drives that just one usb port used for data transfer and to actually power the device sandisk has sensibly opted for the latest usb-c port supporting usb 3.2 gen 2x2 and that theoretically boasts up to 20 gigabits per second of bandwidth but it's also backwards compatible with usb 3 and usb 2 devices albeit with a reduction in speed rather than supplying a single cable with an adapter for older ports as it did on older extreme drives sandisk now supplies the extreme pro with two cables one ending in the usb-c plug and the other ending in usb a much more convenient while the latest xtreme pro drives are pocket sized they have grown noticeably compared to the previous extreme models here's the newer extreme pro on the left next to the plane extreme on the right which measures just 50 mil wide 96 mil tall 9mm thick and is half the weight at 40 grams you'll also notice the cutout shape is different and smaller and that the usb port on the older model is positioned to one side compared to the middle of the new one and the older model also has a slower usb interface too size and cutouts aside one of the most obvious identifiers between them is the orange stripe around the edge of the newer extreme pro models these design cues all help you to find the right model although remember the xtreme pro is also available in two versions rated at 1050 or 2000 megabyte per second speeds so you'll need to check their boxes too not only is the pro model now supplied with two cables but they're longer than the cable supplied with the older extreme now some complained about the short length of the old cable but i actually preferred it for dangling less obviously from a laptop than the new one does technically speaking shorter cables are sometimes necessary for the fastest speeds but i tried the new drive with both cables and measured no difference in performance at least on my computer okay now for performance with the latest xtreme pro version 2 employing an nvme ssd with claimed read and write speeds up to megabytes per second i tested it on my fastest computer a 2018 macbook pro which has usb 3.1 gen2 ports rated up to 10 gigabits per second which works out at per second now given that this is a theoretical bandwidth and in practice you're more likely to be closer to achieving a thousand megabytes per second it still represents a significant bottleneck for the pro version to drive allowing only roughly half its maximum data rate to pass but don't assume a newer macbook will be any better as the usb ports on the late 2020 models are still restricted to 10 gigabit per second speeds indeed if you want a 20 gigabit per second usb port at the time i made this review you'll need to buy a usb 3.2 gen 2x2 expansion card for a desktop pc which specifically quotes support for 20 gigabits per second or wait for laptops with faster ports to arrive it pays to check the specs of your system very carefully especially as the labeling of usb ports can be very confusing across multiple runs on the pro version 2 drive the blackmagic disk speed test utility measured between 940 and 960 megabytes per second for both reads and writes whether using the one gigabyte or five gigabyte stress size these scores are definitely being restricted by my macbook's usb ports but it's still roughly double what i measured from the earlier extreme version one drive which itself was already four times faster than the spinning hard disk in my wd my passport four terabyte drive in real life tests i timed the transfer of a 2.99 gigabyte folder containing 177 jpeg and raw images the earlier extreme version one drive took 14 seconds to write the data from my mac onto the drive then following a restart seven seconds to read it back again from the drive in contrast the extreme pro version 2 took just over 5 seconds to write the data to the drive and just under 4 to read it back again just compare those times to my 4 terabyte passport drive which took 44 seconds to write the data and 28 to read them back the extreme pro version 2 really is a very fast drive even when it's restricted to about half its maximum speed i also tried copying a single large video file measuring 10.68 gigabytes which took 54 seconds to write and 20 seconds to read when using the older extreme version 1 drive but a mere 11.2 seconds to write or 11.4 seconds to read on the extreme pro version 2.Again the almost identical figures for read and write performance of just under a thousand megabytes per second indicate a bottleneck in this case my usb port but it still remains much faster than my older extreme version one i also tried editing entire 4k video projects directly from the drive with no performance issues on both my mac laptop as well as my ipad pro running lumafusion as seen here although this was also possible with the older xtreme version 1 drives it also worked well when connected to my samsung galaxy s20 phone for copying large amounts of photos or videos to and from it i didn't get the chance to try on a games console or a cinema camera though so i'd recommend checking for compatibility with specific models during sustained use i found the extreme pro version 2 drive generally became warmer than my previous extreme version 1 drive not exactly hot but definitely very warm sandisks employed an aluminium chassis to act as a heatsink and it's certainly transferring the heat evenly across the case unlike the previous extreme version 1 which only tended to warm up around the concentrated port area okay now for my verdict as a photographer i love using portable ssds as quick compact and robust backup drives at the end of the day shooting i copy the card onto my laptop then duplicate it onto the ssd i'd then keep the ssd in a separate location to my computer such as a hotel safe when i was out with the laptop the speed of the drive means you'll never neglect performing backups the size means you'll always have it with you when you need it and the robustness means you don't need to treat it with kid gloves i can literally throw it into a bag or slip it in a pocket and not worry as a video editor i often find myself with giant folders of footage quickly consuming the internal drive of my laptop previously i'd free up space by copying projects i wasn't currently working on to portable drives or network storage but the slow speed meant a huge folder could take so long to copy it felt more like archiving but with the ssd i can rapidly transfer huge folders back and forth it's so fast i can even work directly from the drive whether it's storing regular assets an output render or even an entire project now the load disk space warning no longer means i'm out of action wherever i worked i also liked how the drive was small and light enough to happily dangle from the end of its cable without demanding flat desk space to rest on you can even pick up your laptop with it still dangling without troubling the drive or your paws it's so convenient when you're literally working off your lap in a cafe or on a plane or train and you need to get up or move around but if you're getting a sense of deja vu it's because i made the same conclusion at the end of my extreme version 1 drive review all of the above is perfectly possible with an ssd rated at 550 megabytes per second it's just that the newer pro version 2 drive can theoretically do it four times faster still thing is though at the time i made this review most people won't be able to enjoy the top speed as their usb port simply won't be fast enough not only will you need a usb 3.2 gen 2x2 port but one that specifically supports 20 gigabit per second speeds and that rules out even the late 2020 apple laptops so for many of us the pro version 2 drive will only work out twice as fast as the 550 megabyte per second models so it's only going to be delivering half of its potential speed again for most of the people out there indeed since sandisk understandably charges a premium for the pro version 2 performance i wish they'd also make a thunderbolt 3 version as this interface supports up to 40 gigabits per second even on macs sold from 2016 but by adopting the latest usb spec instead there's not a lot of devices able to yet exploit the top speed of course this does give the drive some degree of future proofing but the simple fact is most people won't see a difference between the extreme pro version 2 and cheaper drives rated up to a thousand megabytes per second like the extreme pro version one drives or extreme version 2 models ultimately backing up your data whether it's photos videos music or plain old documents is critically important and a crucial part of that process is transporting the backup to a different location after all fire flood and theft will rarely take one drive but leave the backup next to it unharmed this is why portable drives are so useful they can store and transport a large quantity of data quickly and easily just one usb cable will transfer the data and power the drive and once complete they're small enough to slip into a jacket or even trouser pocket their portability also makes them great for providing more storage for laptop owners who have filled their internal drives but aren't quite ready for a system upgrade yet the earlier sandisk it stream portable ssd quickly found itself part of my workflow thanks to its speed size and robustness all of which meant i never had an excuse not to use it it became my go-to backup when i'm out and about and a convenient way to boost the storage on my laptop when working on big projects the newer xtreme pro version 2 provided the same flexibility but with the benefit faster speeds and while my own devices meant i only enjoyed half of its theoretical performance it remains the fastest drive i've tested as such unless i expected to buy a computer with a 20 gigabit usb port in the future i'd personally save money and buy a model rated at a thousand megabytes per second like the extreme pro version one or extreme version two instead but the bottom line here is regardless of model i've come to regard a portable ssd as an essential accessory and the santis range provides an ever-growing choice of speeds capacities and prices so buy the one that best matches your needs but do check the specs of the drive and your ports very carefully okay that's the end of another review i hope you found it useful if you did the best way to support me is to subscribe to my channel it really does help a lot and if you really really found it useful you could treat me to a coffee or treat yourself to some camera labs merchandise or perhaps a copy of my in-camera photography book and there's links for all of those including checking the latest prices on the sandisk drives in the pin common and the description below so let me know in the comments what you think of this drive and in particular what solution you're using for your backups and whether you've got a computer that can exploit 20 gigabit per second drives thanks again for watching and i'll see you next time bye

If you're looking to buy an external SSD for your Mac, then there's a couple things that you need to consider.And in this video, I'm going to help you understand everything to know about SSDs and Mac computers, some of the models I recommend, and which ones I would buy depending on the type of work I was getting it for.I'm going to have links to buy all these drives in the description below.Some of these drives and enclosures have been provided to me for free, but these are the ones that I have tested extensively and use every day to make sure that they're the best possible options for you to buy.The first thing to note is there's two main types of drives.There's prebuilt and there's enclosures.So an enclosure is going to come without a drive in it.You're going to have to get your own drive and install it, which is really easy to do.But there's some issues that you can run into with heat buildup and also with certain drives not being compatible or recommended with certain enclosures.So I personally recommend most users just buy a prebuilt drive.Then when you look at prebuilt drives, there's a couple different speed classes.The first level is the USB 3.0 speed.So those are drives like the Crucial X9 Pro or the Samsung T7.These are typically going to hit speeds of about 950 megabytes a second and going to be good for most day-to-day work and still be very fast for most users.You can totally edit photos, videos on them, and do a ton of work with these.But if you're running them with intensive video editing tasks, you will notice they start to bog down a little bit over time.I mostly recommend these for offloading files when you're done with projects or also if you just need a good solid SSD, but without the top speeds.Just know if you're doing intensive video work, these will start to slow down on you over time.The next class of drives I'm not even going to recommend is the USB 3.0 2x2 drives.Those promise speeds up to 2000 megabytes a second, but the problem is that speed rating is not compatible with Mac.For whatever reason, the way Apple Silicon works, it will not support those faster speeds, so they'll run at the same exact speed as their slower USB 3.0 counterparts.So if you get something like the Crucial X10 Pro or the Samsung T9, they're the exact same speed as the Crucial X9 Pro and the Samsung T7.You can get those if you find them at a good deal, but you're not going to get any speed boosts.The next class up is USB 4.0 and Thunderbolt 3.0 and Thunderbolt 4.0 drives.These support speeds all the way up to about 3000 or 3200 megabytes a second, and they're very fast.Most of the drives that come in this speed class are also professional rated drives, so they're going to come with features like aluminum cooling cores, and they're going to be designed to work all day long because they put a specific SSD into the specific enclosure and controller, and they're a little bit more rugged than some of the ones that you can build on your own.I really like the SanDisk Professional Pro G40 and the OLED.The Oyen U34 Bolt.These are both very fast, but the Oyen is a little faster than the Pro G40, but the.Pro G40 is slimmer and feels a little more rugged.The SanDisk you can get from one, two, or four terabytes, but the Oyen you can get all the way up to eight terabytes, which is amazing to have eight terabytes of storage in an external enclosure like this.So I really love both of these drives.I use the Oyen as my main daily editing drive because it's fast and works well even running it all day long.The next speed class is Thunderbolt 5 and USB 4.0 V2.These support speeds all the way up to about 6,000 megabytes a second, and it's just insane how fast these drives are.These are drives like the OWC Envoy Ultra.I haven't gotten to test that one out yet.I've heard it's great, but the main issue it has is the cable is built in, so if there's ever any issue with the cable, then you're just out of luck with that drive.I do have the Acasys TB501 Pro enclosure, and I'm hitting speeds of about 6,000 megabytes a second on the right side and about 5,500 on the read side.Just note to get those Thunderbolt 5 speeds, you're going to need a computer that has the M4 Pro or the M4 Max chip, so that's the Mac Mini.M4 Pro, the Mac Studio M4, or also the new M4 Max or M4 Pro MacBook Pro.Any other computer is just going to default to the fastest possible speed that the computer supports.So if we're talking about SSD enclosures, I've had great luck with these by Acasys.I've also got this Satechi USB4 enclosure I really recommend.This is the one I typically use when I want an external SSD enclosure, and to set one of these up, all you have to do is get yourself an NVMe and you just insert it at an angle like this and you move the little rubber stopper out of the way and now you've got this.SSD.If you want, you can also always swap the NVMe out if you ever want to get a bigger or smaller one or you can use multiple NVMes with one enclosure if you want to.Sometimes you can save money by going with an enclosure and an NVMe, but they're really just a little bit cheaper than buying a pre-built one.The main reason I recommend a pre-built over something like the Satechi or the.Acasys enclosures is because these companies can't test every single NVMe with their enclosure and ensure top possible performance.So if you need the top possible performance, that's when I really recommend a pre-built, especially one like the Sandisk Professional or the Oyen U34 Bolt because those are just top of the line drives.Now that we've talked about all these drives and enclosures, which one should you buy?The first thing you need to ask yourself is what capacity do you need?Do you need one, two, or four terabytes?If you're doing a lot of video work, that's when I really recommend getting a four terabyte.If you're doing some video work, and a lot of photos, that's when the two terabyte is going to come in handy.Or if you're doing a lot of music production, that's when two terabytes or four terabytes is going to be a great option as well.If you're just getting into the world of SSDs and you need a way to expand your max storage, one terabyte would be a great starting option.But for most users, I recommend getting two terabytes just so you have the extra storage space.Then once you've decided your capacity, decide what speed class you want.If you just need something to offload files to at pretty fast speeds, if you're doing light to moderate editing on it, that's when something like the Crucial X9 Pro or the Samsung T7 are going to be perfectly fine options for you.They're plenty fast for most day-to-day use.If you're doing heavy editing and you need sustained performance, that's when I really recommend the SanDisk Professional Pro G40 or the Oyen U34 Bolt to get very fast speeds and very great sustained workload.If you need the fastest possible speeds, that's when you could get the OWC Envoy Ultra.But for most users, you're really not going to need those Thunderbolt 5 speeds, and it's only compatible with a select number of computers.If you want to build out your own Hey everybody it's la insideman we're taking a look today at the seagate one touch ssd this is a portable solid-state drive that connects to your computer with a usb type-c port we've looked at these from other brands and now we're going to give seagate a good look here but before we get into this i do want to let in the interest of full disclosure that this came in free of charge from seagate however all the opinions you're about to hear are my own no one is paying for this review nor has anyone reviewed or approved what you're about to see before it was uploaded so let's get into it now and see what this usb hard drive is all about now the price point on this comes in at around 75 dollars for the 500 gigabyte version that's the one that we have here the one terabyte version from a price perspective is probably the sweet spot as it is with many of these portable ssds that one comes in at 135 there are three colors available we've got the white and gray one here it's got a nice little texture on the side a little metal here at the top it is not waterproof but it is solid state so if you drop it it will likely survive the fall unlike a mechanical drive that is a lot more delicate it just has a single usb type-c port on here to connect to your computer the computer will power the drive if you don't have a usbc equipped computer you have a cable in the box to accommodate that which will go from the usbc on the back here to a standard usb a and then they also have a usbc to usbc cable now one thing to note about usb speeds is that even though ports might look the same they perform differently so what you need to make sure to get the full performance out of this drive is that you're plugging into a gen 2 port typically your computer will have the port labeled like this and if you see that 10 on there that's the port that you're going to want to connect this drive to for the best performance but it will work with any usb port but some ports just might be slower than others now if your computer doesn't have the little usb label by its ports you might have to consult with the manual or go to the support page and then you've got some computers like my macbook pro here that have thunderbolt which is a faster technology but compatible with usb and if you have thunderbolt ports this will run at the full performance because thunderbolt supports that 10 gig usb speed now i've got the drive connected up to my mac and what i'm going to do real quick is just copy over a 1.7 gigabyte video file that we can watch on a few other devices here so drive now what i'm going to do real quick is run a speed test this is the black magic disk speed test and this tests how fast it can write and read to the drive sequentially and what i'm going to do per second which is pretty fast but i've seen faster at this price point and you can see here the reads are a little bit quicker and i'm going to let this test run for a few minutes just to see if it is able to maintain that speed and if it is we will move on and see some other benchmarks all right so i let it run for a few minutes i also changed it to a different port to see if that impacted speed at all but it was pretty much holding steady here at about 800 megabytes per second sequentially and again that's a very good rate of performance for a consumer ssd like this but again i did see slightly faster results on this test with other drives on this very same macbook now earlier i also ran the crystal disk mark test on a windows computer upstairs and you can see that one did a little better now if you direct your attention to the right hand column you will see that its sequential write speed was a little faster on the window side 871 megabytes per second and its sequential read speed on the windows machine came in at 975 megabytes per second where i think this drive really shines is in its random right performance and if you look on the scores below those two top ones you'll see how well it does on random reads and writes and it did well across the board but it really shines again on the right side of things and if you look here compared to some of its peers in the marketplace you'll see that it does much better for example versus the samsung t7 writing at 272.4 megabytes per second versus 143 and it also does well on some of the other tests that the crystal disk mark throws at it for that random read and write performance the random reads aren't bad either as you can see here so i think this drive will perform quite well in a number of different environments including gaming loading up an operating system anything where you're really pinging the drive randomly for bits of data that you might want to read from it or write to it so all in a nicely performing drive even if it's sequential reads and writes are a little bit off versus some of its competitors now you can also plug it into things that are not computers so i've got my ipad out here this is the ipad mini that has the usb type-c port and most of the new ipads now will have one of these ports and as you can see here my seagate drive is showing up and i can click on the video that we uploaded to the drive a little bit earlier nice and loud here and you can see the video is playing just fine off of the solid state drive here so no problem there now one thing that i tried a little bit earlier was plugging it into my iphone iphones have a lightning to usb adapter that you can use to connect external storage unfortunately my iphone said the drive was using too much power so it didn't work with the iphone but it does work with the ipads that have usb type-c connectors on board and it will work with those natively and it will appear in the apple files app and you can move files back and forth to it with apps that are compatible with the apple file system now things get a little more complicated on the android side the reason is is that when you get the drive it is formatted with a file system called exfat this works on the pc and the mac and it works on some android phones but not all so for example i've got a galaxy s8 phone here from samsung and when i plug the drive in and load up the file app here you can see that we are able to jump in and play back that video that we copied to it from the mac a little bit earlier no problem but what happens when we take the drive out and plug it into a google pixel phone well we're going to get an error message here if i can turn off the assistant there and it's going to tell me that it can't recognize the drive when i plug it in or in this case nothing happens at all if i pull down my notifications here it says it's got an issue with the drive and it's going to ask me to format it the reason is is that google phones and some other android devices are not paying for the exfat license so when you plug in an exfat drive it can't work now if i formatted the drive it would work fine but if i had data on here and formatted it with this phone it would erase all of that data so for some android phones you might have to format the drive for fat32 which is readable whereas exfat is not now seagate though has a solution to this problem with an app that they have in the google play store it is called seagate ssd touch and this will only work with their drives and if i connect the drive now and open up their app see it's already prompted me to do that what'll happen now is that i can read the file system and move files back and forth even though this is formatted for exfat so it's going to take a other apps if i need to and then of course i can play back the video file here that we added to the drive on the mac a little bit earlier now this exfat problem is not unique to seagate as these drives get bigger these are the types of file systems that these drives want to use but my advice would be if you have an android phone plug it in first and see if it gets recognized you don't need the app if it does get recognized but if your phone doesn't support exfat and you want to leave the native format of the drive intact you will need to use the app or format it for fat32 now the app does add a few useful utilities to your android phone so for example you can have it make a backup whenever it is plugged in there's also a file syncing feature which might be useful for keeping your photo library backed up for example so there are some things that are of use if you do use the app but again if your phone recognizes the drive when you plug it in or you format it for a file system for the next generation titles so these things are still useful on next-gen consoles but you can only play the older generation games on it nothing is simple these days unfortunately so overall i found this to be a very solid offering from seagate that's comparable to its competitors i was very pleased with the random read and write performance it definitely edged out a few of the other drives Welcome to critical reviews today we're diving into the Seagate OneTouch SSD a product that promises portability and speed but may leave you with more questions than answers let's explore why this drive might not be the best choice for your storage needs reason number five the drive security features are a hassle to set up password protection you have to register the device and download seates toolkit software this means giving away your personal information just to keep your data safe not exactly a great trade-off right reason number four the performance can be inconsistent while it boast speeds up to 1,30 mbes per second some users reported significantly slower transfer rates especially when using certain USB ports you might find yourself waiting longer than expected to move your files reason number three the toolkit software is a nightmare user have complained about its clunky interface and lack of clear instructions it can be frustrating to navigate and if you accidentally cancel a backup you might lose your progress without any warning reason number two limited compatibility while it works with Windows and Mac users have noted that it can be a hassle to set up on Linux systems if you're part of the Linux Community you might want to look elsewhere for your external storage needs and finally reason number one reliability concerns several users reported that their drives failed after a short period of use with some lasting only 2 years for a product priced at around $100 you'd expect better longevity so is it worth the investment you might want to think twice in conclusion while the Seagate OneTouch SSD has its perks such as portability and decent speed when it works the drawbacks might outweigh the benefits for many users thanks for tuning in to critical reviews don't forget to like And subscribe for more honest product reviews

Choosing the right external  hard drive can be tricky,   especially when you’re comparing options  like the WD Black P10 Game Drive, the   Seagate Portable 5TB, or the WD Elements 6TB.  Today, we’ll break down the key features,   repairability, durability, and performance  of these drives to help you decide which   one is the best for your needs.Let’s  start by disassembling the WD Elements.  By taking them apart, we can identify the hard  drive model (providing exact specifications)   and examine the circuit board design, which  reveals the engineering behind the product,   such as connector arrangements.This is important  because it shows how repairable the device is.  The WD Elements drive has an  all-plastic enclosure.Inside,   it houses a 2.5-inch hard drive, Model Number:  WD60NDZW-11C8XS0, with a rotational speed of 4800   RPM.It uses SMR (Shingled Magnetic Recording)  technology, optimized for environments with lower   write speeds, such as archiving or media storage.  While it technically operates on the SATA bus,   physical access to the SATA interface is  unavailable, as the circuit board features only a   USB 3.0 Micro-B connector.This design negatively  impacts repairability, as I’ll explain shortly.For comparison, I analyzed  another external hard drive,   the WD EasyStore 5TB (model from 2019),  which is similar to the WD Elements.   These two drives are nearly identical, both  using the same USB Micro-B connector on the   circuit board.However, the EasyStore drive has a  faster rotational speed of 5400 RPM.Despite being   five years older, this model demonstrates  better performance due to the higher RPM.  Next, let’s disassemble the WD Black P10 Game  Drive.For this, you’ll need a 2mm hex screwdriver   to remove the four screws securing the top cover.  While the requirement for a tool adds complexity,   opening the WD Black P10 is still easier than  disassembling drives with internal latches.  The top cover is made of metal, but the hard  drive casing is primarily plastic.Unfortunately,   this design doesn't provide shock  resistance, as the plastic casing   absorbs the impact during a fall or damage.This  choice prioritizes aesthetics over durability.  The WD Black P10 is equipped with a 2.5-inch  hard drive, Model Number: WD50NMZW-59BCBS0, with   a rotational speed of 4800 RPM.Internally, it  is almost identical to the WD Elements drive,   featuring the same USB Micro-B connector and  a rotational speed.Compared to the EasyStore,   the WD Black P10 rotational speed is slower,  making it less performant in this regard.  Finally, let’s open the Seagate Portable  drive, which proved the most difficult to   disassemble.The internal latches are  designed in such a way that some may   break during the process.The case and top cover  are entirely plastic.When opening this drive,   use a plastic tool to avoid damaging the  circuit board, which is positioned facing up.  The Seagate Portable houses a 2.5-inch hard drive,  Model Number: ST5000LM000, with a rotational   speed of 5400 RPM.The critical distinction  between this drive and the WD Black P10 and   WD Elements lies in its design.The Seagate drive  includes a removable USB Micro-B adapter board,   providing direct access to the SATA interface.  This design offers significant advantages:  1.Data Access in Case of Damage: If  the USB Micro-B connector is damaged,   you can remove the drive from its enclosure  and connect it directly to your PC via SATA   or use a USB-to-SATA adapter.This bypasses the  need for a functional USB connection, making   data recovery straightforward and cost-effective.2.Compatibility with Professional Data Recovery   Tools: Recovery tools are designed to work  with SATA interfaces.The Seagate Portable’s   removable board simplifies the process, while  WD drives with embedded USB interfaces require   expensive modifications for SATA access,  increasing recovery costs and complexity.  The removable board design of the Seagate drive  allows DIY enthusiasts or IT professionals to   troubleshoot and potentially recover data using  available data recovery tools.WD drives, lacking   this flexibility, often force users to rely on  specialized and expensive recovery services.  This design difference makes the Seagate Portable  drive a better choice for users concerned about   long-term data accessibility and recovery options,  especially in cases involving hardware failures.  In terms of case height, all three drives are  roughly the same.The WD Black P10 and Seagate   Portable cases have identical heights, while the  WD Elements case is slightly smaller.However,   the WD Black P10 case is wider, making it  the largest overall.The Seagate Portable   is mid-sized, and the WD Elements  drive has the smallest footprint.  As for the warranty on drives, the WD  Elements comes with a 2-year warranty.  WD Black P10 Game Drive  comes with a 3-year warranty.  The Seagate Portable Limited Warranty  varies by region and location of purchase:  Asia-Pacific: The warranty is the longest  here, at 3 years, providing extended   coverage for customers in this region.Europe, the Middle East, and Africa:   The warranty extends 2 years.For Americas: The warranty is   valid for 1 year, covering countries  in North, Central, and South America.  Additionally, Seagate provides Rescue Data  Recovery Services for data recovery in case of   hardware failure, accidental deletion, or physical  damage.This service is included with the Seagate   Portable Drive for a specified period, typically  aligned with the warranty, at no extra cost.To evaluate performance, I conducted  tests using CrystalDiskMark version 8.0.6.  First Test: I used the standard 1  Gibibyte benchmark, which provides   a reliable estimate of drive performance.Second Test: A 16 Gibibyte benchmark was   used to simulate real-world scenarios involving  large files, as larger drives often deliver more   accurate results under these conditions.The test results showed that the Seagate   Portable excelled in most performance metrics,  outperforming other drives.It emerged as the   top choice for sequential read and write  speeds, which are crucial for efficiently   transferring large files.The WD Elements  is suitable for scenarios involving frequent   small file operations.The WD Black P10 lags  in performance compared to the other drives,   particularly in sequential read and write speeds.After a thorough analysis, let's sum it up.  Seagate Portable: This drive stands  out as the best overall performer,   delivering the fastest sequential read and write  speeds, which are crucial for efficiently handling   large files.Its removable USB adapter board  provides direct access to the SATA interface,   a critical feature for easy data recovery and  long-term accessibility.The Seagate Portable   is the ideal choice for users prioritizing  speed, versatility, and data recovery options.WD Elements: This model excels in random write  tasks, making it a suitable option for scenarios   involving frequent small file operations.While  its performance is slightly behind the Seagate   Portable in sequential tasks, its compact size  and solid random write capabilities make it a   dependable choice for general-purpose storage,  especially for archiving or media use.However,   its limited repairability due to the  embedded USB interface is a drawback.WD Black P10: Designed with gamers in mind, it  lags in performance compared to the other drives,   particularly in sequential read and write speeds,   it may appeal to users who value  aesthetics and build quality over   peak performance.The only advantage of this  hard drive is the 3-year warranty period.The only common feature of these external  hard drives is their durability.Hard drive   technology is inherently fragile, and the plastic  cases make them susceptible to damage from falls,   compromising their overall durability.Conclusion:  In my opinion, the best external hard drive  is the Seagate Portable.And here is why.  The Seagate Portable emerges  as the best all-around option,   offering an excellent balance of speed,  reliability, and repairability.However,   if your use case involves heavy small  file transfers, the WD Elements is a   strong contender.For those who prioritize  design, the WD Black P10 could be the right   fit.I left links to these external hard  drives in the video description below.  Anyway, your choice should depend on your  specific needs—whether it’s performance,   data recovery flexibility, or warranty period.Thank you for watching this detailed comparison   of external hard drives.If you found this  review helpful, please give it a thumbs up   and subscribe for more in-depth reviews, teardown  guides, and repair tutorials.Your support helps   us create more content like this.Thank you for  watching HowFixit, and see you in the next video!

Today we're taking a closer look at the WD 6 TBTE My Passport Portable external hard drive.A sleek, reliable storage solution designed for anyone who needs extra space for backups, media, or important files.Whether you're a student, creative professional, or just someone who wants to keep their digital life organized, this device promises to deliver both capacity and peace of mind.Let's dive into what makes it stand out.One of the first things you'll notice about the My Passport is its compact portable design.Despite packing a hefty six stoutery of storage, it's surprisingly lightweight and fits easily in a backpack or even a large pocket.But the real magic lies in its features.It comes with 256-bit AES hardware encryption and password protection, ensuring your data stays secure even if the drive falls into the wrong hands.Plus, WD's built-in backup software offers defense against ransomware, a rare and valuable feature in external drives.Compatibility is a breeze thanks to USB 3.1, USB 3.0 support, delivering fast transfer speeds, though you'll need a compatible port to take full advantage.In real world use, the My Passport performs admirably.Transferring large files like 4K videos or extensive photo libraries is smooth and quick with minimal lag.The drive stays cool and quiet even during prolonged use, making it ideal for onthe-go professionals.The included WDD backup software is userfriendly, allowing automatic backups with just a few clicks.However, it's worth noting that the drive is formatted for Windows out of the box, so Mac users will need to reformat it.A minor inconvenience, but something to keep in mind.Now, let's talk about the competition.Compared to similar drives like the Seagate Backup Plus or Lassi Rugged, the My Passport holds its own with superior security features and a more polished design.While Seagate might offer slightly better value in terms of pure storage per dollar, WD's ransomware protection and encryption give it an edge for users prioritizing data safety.To get the most out of this drive, here are a few tips.First, always safely eject it to avoid data corruption.Second, take advantage of the WD security software to set up password protection right away.And if you're using it for backups, schedule regular updates to keep your files current.In summary, the WD6 Tetabyte My Passport is a fantastic choice for anyone needing reliable, secure, and portable storage.It's especially well suited for photographers, videographers, or anyone handling sensitive data.While the Mac reformatting step and the lack of USBC might be slight drawbacks for some, the overall package is hard to beat.If you're in the market for a high-capacity external drive that balances performance, security, and portability, this one's a winner.Thanks for tuning in and happy storing.Catch [Music]

strides in  terms of the disk capacity.This challenge could   have been one of the reasons why WD made some of  the decisions that I'm going to come to.Secondly,   going back some years I have been better job in terms  of disk reliability and quality than they had be   previously.As well as leading the way in terms  of capacity, and price to capacity ratios.But   the real kicker for me was what happened with WD  and their use of SMR drives or Shingled Magnetic   recording technology on their NAS Drives.And  ultimately this was the deciding factor for me.   To give you some background there are a number of  technologies that disk manufacturers have used to   increase data density.Since around the mid-2000s  this has been done primarily using something   called PMR or perpendicular magnetic recording, and  this is also known as CMR or conventional magnetic   recording.This technology simply places disk  tracks next to each other on the disk platters   and writing the magnetic polarity into the  recording layer perpendicular to the write   head.The innovation for increased capacity here  was really just increasing platter counts in the   disk and also increasing areal density by making  these tracks and write heads smaller.But this was   running into limiting factors around how small  these tracks could really be.One way to overcome   this, was to use something called Shingled Magnetic  Recording, and with this approach the tracks   partially overlap and this means that the size of  the right head and the granularity of the writing   doesn't need to be so small.So this is fine but  it means that as you write data you're actually   partially overwriting data on neighboring tracks  and you need to go and rewrite that data on those   tracks that may have been overwritten.So this does  have an impact to sustain and write performance, if   you want to write one Megabyte of data you may  need to rewrite several other Megabytes on those   neighboring tracks to retain data consistency  and stability.This could have a particularly   bad impact on very large writes especially things  like large RAID rebuilds.If your writes are fairly   small and not continuous then this doesn't have  a serious impact, so it's a good technology for   smaller writes and lower frequencies to reduce  cost.You can mitigate the limitations of SMR by   using larger disk caches, now as data is written to  disk the write performance impact can be offset by   writing to the larger cache which is fast and the  disk can then catch up from the cache.However if   you're performing a lot of large writes then the  cache will fill and then you'll have delays on I/O   to the disk as it performs these rewrites and  you get lower performance.And this is why these   types of disks are less suitable for NAS use cases  where large files are being written often.But the   use of SMR wasn't the reason I stopped using WD  specifically, I could have just stopped using those   disks.The reason was that WD went to great  lengths to actively hide this from customers.Not   only was it not documented but if you went looking  and asking the right questions you couldn't easily   find out which disk had CMR and which had SMR.They  went to significant effort to hide what   they were doing, presumably so they could keep up  with the price to capacity of their competitors.   I originally used CMR Western Digital Red six  Terabyte disks at the time in my Synology DS2415   plus NAS but they replaced these disks with SMR  versions quietly and the only way I can identify   which was which was by looking at the cache size.  The CMR drives had the part numbered WD60WFRX   and this has 64 MB of cache and the SMR drives  were WD60EFAX and they had 256 Mb.So on face   value the larger cache makes them look like better  drives.But that cache was there to mitigate the   limitations of SMR write performance.Eventually,  and it really did take too long, WD were called out   on this very publicly and they got a great deal  of customer backlash.This resulted in them keeping   the WD Red range and using SMR, and then creating  a WD Red Plus range and using CMR having to send this   disk back and all the previous problems, this is  when I decided that I was done with WD at this   point.Now would I go back to WD?Yes, I would.But  they really need to do some work to catch up on   their price to capacity and also there's definitely  some trust that needs to be rebuilt.And honestly   my experience with the Western Digital Red range  has been really good, I've been using the sandis card for a few years now to shoot photos and videos and while it's been fantastic there's definitely one thing I think you should know before you actually get it so first the speed is incredible with shot speeds up to 90 megabytes per second and transfer speeds up to 170 megabytes per second it it handles high resolution photos and 4K UHD videos really well so the design is robust and it's really built to last it's temperature proof waterproof proof and it's even x-ray proof so it's perfect for traveling now one of the best features is its large capacity this is the 512 GB version and it provides a lot of space for extensive photo shoots or long video sessions really reducing the need to frequently offload files which is great however there's one thing to keep in mind this card's high-speed capabilities might be overkill for older devices that really can't take full advantage of its performance so if you're using it with older equipment you might not see the full benefits of its speed overall this is one of the only SD cards I use due to its durability and quality and I highly recommend it to anyone looking to capture high quality images and videos reliably

Is the Lexar Professional 1800x SDXC UHS-II Gold 256GB a perfect balance between speed, capacity, warranty, and price with its large capacity and high-speed UHS-II certification?To start off, let us talk about its performance.Based on our extensive testing, the Lexar Professional Gold card delivered solid speed; matching its rated linear figures of 270MB/s read and 180MB/s write in our benchmarks.When it comes to small and random file writes in ATTO and Crystal Disk Mark, Lexar's latest SDXC card delivered exceptional numbers thanks to the flash memory's high IOPS performance.Simply put, its performance is excellent.You can buy an even faster 2000x card from the company, but the 1800x version we are reviewing today is plenty fast.Capacity is exactly what is advertised -- its 256GB storage capacity is a lot of space.It is too bad the 1800x SDXC UHS-II Gold lineup does not come in larger 512GB or even 1TB variants, but you already know what you are getting into.For about $110 at press time, the Lexar Professional 1800x SDXC UHS-II Gold 256GB offers tons of bang for your buck if you are looking for a speedy card.As for the warranty?The limited lifetime warranty is the cherry on the cake to ensure you are covered for years to come.Let me know in the comments what you think of this card.

fast or maybe even slightly faster if you get the one I mentioned above.Know how big the files are and know how many frames your camera can shoot per second.Then multiply these two values to get the amount of megabytes per second that the camera will produce.That is the maximum right speed of the card that you can make use of.Anything faster is wasted money.Any card that is slower than that may limit your camera's shooting capabilities if you need to capture many frames per second.For photography, do check the specification of the card in regard to the actual right speed, but do keep in mind that manufacturers give you the value for the best possible circumstances for that right speed.So subtract about 20%.Don't go by the classes.For example, the Lexa 1066 by that gave us the best result on the Canon T7i wrote at 104 megaby pers, but it is a V30 card, which would make us believe it can only write at 30 megabytes pers.The Vclass is only important for video.If you shoot video, check your manual because it usually lists the exact standard needed for each of the video settings that are possible with that camera.No matter how fast the card is, if it doesn't support a video standard, the camera might tell you that it cannot record on it.That's a software thing rather than a hardware limitation.As you can see, my approach to creating videos is research and real life experience.I don't just repeat what has been said countless times.And that often leads to new insights.If you like that approach, I bet you will appreciate my video about the real difference of crop versus fullframe cameras.

Have you ever looked at a CF Express type a card and you wondered what do all of those numbers and icons mean well today I'm going to break it all down so you'll know exactly what you're looking at when you pick up one for your [Music] camera first up let's talk about capacity the number that you'll see most prominently is the card's storage capacity usually in gigabytes this tells you how much data the card in store for example this card has 160 GB written on it meaning it could store up to 160 GB of photos or video files if you shoot a lot of highquality photos or you're shooting 4K or 8K video you'll want a large capacity card like the 160 GB card next you'll see some speed ratings the first one that you'll notice is the maximum read speed which is often marked as something like 800 gabt per second this tells you just how fast the card can transfer data to your computer so if you're offloading files from your camera to your computer a higher read speed will save you a lot of time then there's the right speed which is equally important especially if you're shooting High bit rate video or you're doing rapid burst of photos this card for instance has a right speed of 700 megabytes per second that's how fast they can save data to the card while you're recording faster right speeds mean your camera won't lag behind when you're capturing all those high resolution shots or your video clips and it's going to clear the buffer a lot faster now of course we can't forget about the CF Express type a logo this identifies the card as part of the CF Express family and the type a means that it's the smallest and the most compact version of the CF Express cards this format is ideal for smaller cameras like Sony's Alpha Series and the FX series and it provides fast performance in a compact size finally the icon of a director slate with a number like for example this one has 400 on a CF Express type a card it refers to the video performance guarantee this number indicates the minimum sustained right speed in megabytes per second that the card can maintain during video recording for example if the number is 400 it means that the card can sustain a minim minimum right speed of 400 megabytes per second during video capture this is important for ensuring Smooth highquality video recording without dropping frames and especially when you're shooting in high resolutions like 4K or 8K or you're shooting at those higher bit rates so the higher the vpg number the more reliable the card is for demanding video work I made a video for Alpha Universe a few years ago explaining what the details mean on an SD card but one of the common questions that I get is whether or not to use an SD card over a CF Express type a card the answer to that is that it depends on what your specific needs are for example let's say that you're a portrait photographer and you're taking photos with a camera like the alpha 7 R5 this camera accepts both CF Express type A and SD cards if you're taking portraits with this camera and you shoot relatively slowly in your sessions the speed that you get on Sony's tough SD cards is should be more than enough for you if however you are shooting a wedding and you plan on shooting thousands of photos you're able to get higher capacity CF Express typ a cards compared to SD cards if you are shooting High frame rates on that camera you'll also want to consider CF Express type A cards since the buffer will actually clear much faster when it comes to any still photography it really all boils down to how fast you're shooting and whether or not you need storage capacities beyond what you can get with an SD card now when it comes to video if you're shooting in a lower bit rate and you don't plan on shooting in 4k or 8K resolutions you'll probably be okay shooting with an SD card if however you want to get the maximum bit rates and the maximum resolution the demands are much higher and it's going to require you to use a memory card that can keep up with that so now that what all of those icons and the details mean you can make a more informed decision based on how you're shooting your Stills in your videos and you can get the performance that you need I'll link that video for you in a moment so that you could check that out if you found this video helpful be sure to leave a like for me I'd really appreciate that and if you have any further questions let me know in the comments section below subscribe to Alpha Universe to stay up todate on the latest news and tutorials and as promised check out the video that you see here on the screen to learn more about Sony's memory cards I'll see you there

﻿The choice of your memory card is probably  one of the most important decisions you have   to make regarding the accessories, because  if you get a wrong one, you might be limited   on how much of your camera's potential you  can use.So we know there are microSD cards,   Sd Cards and also this little guy - CFexpress  Type A card for all the awesome Sony shooters out   there - This is the SE version - just released  from Angelbird.If you haven’t seen it before,   I will tell you why this thing matters and  why you should or should not get one.  And if you don’t know Angelbird yet - it is  my favorite memory card maker from Austria,   I recommend their products because of their  quality and the human touch.I’ve worked on   many projects with them and they did send me  this memory card but I am not asked to make this   video or in any way limited to telling my honest  thoughts, so that out of the way - speed matters.  We all know SD cards and they are totally fine  for most users, but if you have high demands for   write speeds say you do uncompressed RAW burst or  shoot videos in the highest bitrate - the regular   SD cards, even if it's the fastest 300mb/s one  - most likely won't cut it and as soon as you   try to put your camera in the highest bitrate when  shooting videos - this is the error you will get.  Why is that?Well it’s just not fast enough.Even  though 600mb/s bitrate on camera is roughly 75MB/s   and SD Card can handle 300MB/s - the 300 is only  the maximum, not the sustained speed.Therefore,   you gotta use CFExpress cards, which are not  only with bigger capacities, they are also a lot   faster.Funny thing - you can still shoot 600mb/s  on A6700 with the Angelbirds V90 SD card.   I guess the a6700 has better internal processing  and buffer management than the A7IV?Interesting.  Anyways, it’s important to note that there are  CFexpress Type B - really popular cards with   lots of manufacturers, as well as CFexpress Type  A cards.Type A are a lot more rare and just a   few years ago, it was only Sony and Angelbird  who were making them, now, few more companies   have joined the party, but the important part is  that if you shoot a Sony camera, only the TYPE   A will fit you.And not all cameras can use these  cards.If you shoot any of the APSC cameras, this   probably won’t be too relevant for you since these  cameras don’t have the specific CFexpress Type A   card slot which is found on A7IV, on FX6 and  many more.Also not all full frame cameras such   as A7CII have that port, so check it before even  thinking of buying these cards because these cards   are extremely expensive.The SD cards already are,  but these ones are on to another level.Luckily we   have Angelbird and by no means they are budget  producer.Quite the opposite.All their cards   are made in Austria and with every memory card  purchase you get a 3-year extended warranty as   well as individual, in-house tech support and  a free data recovery service that covers both   hardware and software issues.That is very rare.  Nevertheless, the price is very competitive and   always much better than Sony’s Tough cards.On to the new Angelbird card, well first they   came out with the original AV PRO - CFexpress Type  A card and I got this massive capacity 1TB card   with this insane 820mb/s max speed.Now the new  cards have various - smaller capacities which is   great because not everyone wants to invest in the  1TB card.What I have here is a 330 GB version and   there is also a 160 GB version.Both are with the  maximum 820mb/s speed and because they use Stable   Stream technology, these cards ensure consistent  sustained write speed of 650 MB/s throughout the   entire recording (not just at the beginning).So  what it means is that you can do 8K RAW, 4K, and   high frame-rate video at up to 120 fps without any  frame drops, as well as buffer-free raw photos and   image bursts.On top of that these cards are now  officially VPG 200 certified, ensuring top-tier   performance for professional video recording.So there you have it.Check if your camera has   this port, and if you do, consider getting one of  these to be able to shoot in those high bitrates.   If you don’t have that port, your next best  bet is Angelbirds AV PRO SD CARD - just make   sure it is the V90 card.Their capacities go  up to 512 GBs, I have many of them and again,   can’t recommend them enough.Hope you learned something new   about the CFexpress cards and Angelbird,  until next time guys, just keep on creating.

Corsair a name synonymous with highquality computer components offers a range of Premium Ram modules catering to diverse user needs two of its Flagship series The Corsair Vengeance and Corsair Dominator Platinum stand out as top contenders in the world of PC memory in this comparison we'll delve into the features performance and Aesthetics of these two series to help you choose the ram that best suits your requirements also details about the products we speak about will be linked down in the description Corsair is known for its attention to detail in design and both the Vengeance and Dominator Platinum Series embodied this ethos the Vengeance series features a Sleek minimalist design with a lowprofile aluminum heat spreader available in various colors including black white and red Vengeance modules can seamlessly blend into different build themes they also feature RGB versions for users who crave vibrant customizable lighting to complement their system Aesthetics Vengeance RGB modules come equipped with 10 individually addressable RGB LEDs allowing ing for intricate lighting effects the Dominator Platinum Series takes Aesthetics to the next level with its iconic polished aluminum heat spreaders and customizable light bars these modules not only look premium but also exude a sense of sheer quality the Dominator Platinum RGB modules feature an impressive 12 RGB LEDs per module making them a lighting Powerhouse they come with a stylish light bar upgrade kit that allows you to further personalize the appearance of your RAM when it comes to Aesthetics the Dominator series is a standout choice for those who want to showcase their high-end build the choice here depends largely on your personal aesthetic preferences if you favor a clean and minimalist look with the option for RGB lighting Corsair Vengeance might be your go-to choice however if you desire a truly premium appearance and extensive RGB illumination the Corsair Dominator has the upper hand when it comes to Ram performance is a crucial Factor both the Corsair Vengeance and Dominator Platinum Series deliver impressive performance but they differ in certain aspects Vengeance modules are available in various speeds and capacities ranging from 2133 MHz to 4800 MHz ensuring there's an option for every user these modules are built with highquality dram chips and support extreme memory profiles for easy overclocking this makes Vengeance a solid choice for gamers and PC enthusiasts who seek a balance between performance and price Dominator Platinum modules offer similar versatility in terms of speed and capacity with kits ranging from 2400 MHz to 4800 gz what sets the Dominator Platinum Series apart is its exceptional overclocking potential and Tighter timings making it a favorite among enthusiasts and overclockers these modules are handc screened for quality and feature corsair's dual path heat exchange cooling technology ensuring stability even under heavy loads if you're looking for top tier performance with overclocking capabilities the Corsair Dominator series is the clear winner however if you're on a tighter budget and still desire excellent performance Corsair vengeance is a compelling option RGB Ram isn't just about lighting it's also about control and customization both the Vengeance and Dominator provide robust software solutions for this purpose Vengeance RGB modules are managed using corsair's icuee software which offers a userfriendly interface for advanced lighting customization IC also enables synchronization with other Corsair RGB components creating a cohesive lighting experience throughout your system it supports third-party integration allowing you to sync your Ram's lighting with popular games and applications Dominator Platinum RGB modules are also controlled through corsair's icuee software offering similar lighting customization options and synchronization capabilities this means you can seamlessly integrate your Dominator Platinum RGB Ram with other Corsair RGB components for a unified lighting experience both Ram series provide extensive control over your RGB lighting and the choice between them may come down to your overall system setup and existing Corsair components in the battle between Corsair Vengeance and Corsair Dominator Platinum both Ram series offer exceptional performance and Aesthetics your choice should depend on your specific needs budget and aesthetic preferences if you're seeking a balance between performance and price with the option for RGB lighting customization Corsair vengeance is an excellent choice it provides a clean minimalist design that can seamlessly fit into various build themes however if you prioritize premium Aesthetics exceptional overclocking capabilities and a unique polished appearance the Corsair Dominator Platinum Series is the clear winner it caters to enthusiasts who want to showcase their high-end builds while still enjoying top tier Ram performance click on the video on your screen to assess the individual performance of both these memory kits in detail

It's easy to spend a lot of money on the system memory inside your PC £300 £400 perhaps even £500 and the obvious question is what memory should you buy and what do you get in return is it as simple as saying I want 32 GB of ddr5 6000 that will run a XMP for Intel or Expo for AMD and that's the end of the matter well no of course it isn't we've recently spoken to Dan Ragland of Intel and Martine Boonstra of AMD on this exact subject and the videos have gone down rather well so now it's time to actually look at the hardware now we rather cheeky put the question to Corsair we said memory does it much matter and Corsair a company that sells rather a lot of memory said yes it matters a great deal the memory we have here is Corsair Vengeance RGB in white it's a 64 GB kit so 2x 32 GB rate at dddr 55600 CL 36 and it's XMP for Intel we previously featured the dominated titanium first edition 64 GB kit which is DDR 56000 that's cl30 and XO for AMD we also have Dominator Platinum RGB that's a 32 GB kit dddr 57000 cl34 and XMP for Intel and then we have two Corsair Vengeance kits and this brings us to the heart of the matter by ey can you tell me which of these kits is rated at 5200 and which is rated at 6800 and the answer of course is that you can't because the form factor of the modules is identical the heat spreaders are identical it's all about the ic's the integrated circuits under those heat spreaders and while the pricing of these two kits doesn't vary hugely the 5200 is rather slow and old costs £105 whereas the the 6800 is bang up to date and costs £130 you cannot of course be sure that 6800 memory will run successfully in your PC 5200 you can be sure that'll work but will it actually be any good memor is arranged in a grid you have to understand the row and the column configuration in order to address specific cells you also have to refresh those cells memory is not a persistent product unlike say this SSD from Corsair and this gives rise to the somewhat price Alder Lake CPUs from Intel's 12th generation have welcomed ddr5 although not all Alder Lake users have followed suit ddr5 is still in its initial stages and first generation memory kits haven't yet shown off all of its capabilities however well-known companies like g-skill have introduced their premium Trident Z5 lineup for Die Hard fans that desire to buy the best ddr5 has to offer at the moment in its product cycle this video will cover the Trident Z5 RGB and go over its specs and performance for users also details about the products we speak about will be linked down in the description in regards to the design of the Trident Z5 RGB it maintains the Trident theme the two-tone surface of the aluminum heat spreader gives it a more opulent appearance than earlier Trident iterations the spreader on the 1.65 inch tall Trident Z5 RGB is available in matte black or shiny silver depending on user preference Additionally the RGB variations come with a translucent RGB light bar while the non-rgb versions come with a standard plastic bar for the former g-skill comes with 5.8 megabytes of the company's own Trident Z lighting Control software finally the Trident Z5 RGB memory modules work well with Asus aurasync gigabyte RGB Fusion 2.0 and MSI Mystic light sync if that's the motherboard software you choose the 16 gigabyte Trident Z5 RGB memory modules use a single rank configuration and have a capacity for this specific memory kit g-skill selected Samsung's top tier B die integrated circuit enabling it to maintain extremely tight timings at the stated data rate the npec power management integrated circuit has a specific model number you can check in the description below the memory package will initially operate at DDR 54800 with 40 40 76 timings before the XMP 3.0 profile is enabled the Trident Z5 RGB will reach ddr56000 with 36 36 3676 timings and a 1.3 volt d-ram voltage when the switch is turned on the Trident Z5 RGB memory kit completed the application test Suite with the fastest overall speed the majority of the benchmarks were dominated by it the memory kit performed 20 better than the slowest memory kit on the Adobe Photoshop 2022 workload which was especially demanding unexpectedly g-skills memory kit performed poorly in the gaming category with the Trident Z5 RGB placed in the center of the other memory kits in regards to overclocking with the Trident Z5 there is always a chance of harm when running gear outside of the manual factors guidelines this is a general warning when overclocking any device not just memory Samsung informed us that it does not promise an overclocking voltage for its integrated circuits the drain voltage's maximum DC value is 1.4 volts we were able to extract ddr5 6200 with the precise XMP memory timings from the Trident Z5 RGB the memory did however Force us to increase the dram voltage from 1.3 volts to 1.4 volts in conclusion the data speaks for itself one of the fastest ddr5 memory kits available is the g-skill Trident Z5 RGB ddr56000c36 additionally it features the greatest timings a DDR 5 6000 memory kit can offer Trident Z5 RGB memory is able to operate at precise timings even after the ddr56000 specification is reached because of the use of Samsung's B die integrated circuits overall the g-skill Trident C series is a fantastic choice for those looking to upgrade their memory kits to a stronger ddr5 and gain better performance click on the video on your screen to check out other g-skill memory kits like the rip draws x-series to compare the Trident series to its predecessors

These days processors from AMD and Intel May utilize ddr5 RAM and as a result more and more brand new ddr5 memory kits are being sold in shops throughout the world in this market g-skill is a well-known business that produces some of the greatest Ram modules available in this video we will be taking a look at their memory kit the g-skill Trident Z5 Neo ddr56032 gigabyte which is made for the most recent AMD ryzen 7000 family of processors and how smoothly the kit integrates itself into your PC also details about the products we speak about will be linked down in the description the Trident Z5 Neo RGB memory modules were created using the same design principles as the other Trident Z5 models with a matte black exterior a black brushed aluminum inlay and silver highlights the fashionable heat spreader is delivered there is currently no information on whether g-skill will provide the metallic silver trim option for the Trident Z5 Neo RGB memory kits in the future the Neo RGB model shares the same height as its predecessors because g-skill utilizes the same mold for all of its Trident Z5 memory the memory modules therefore measure 1.65 inches in height the Trident Z5 Neo RGB memory modules are topped by a Sleek RGB light bar through the software on your motherboard or the g-skill Trident Z lighting Control software you have total control over the lighting and effects the Trident Z5 Neo RGB is compatible with Asus Aura sync gigabyte RGB Fusion 2.0 MSI Mystic light sync and as rock polychrome sync if you choose the latter option the Trident Z5 Neo RGB memory kit from g-skill consists of two single rank 16 gigabytes ddr5 memory modules the memory module's usage of SK hynix integrated circuits a common option among memory providers is therefore not surprising the power management IC also Bears the zero d9bj1u designation indicating that it is a product of Rick Tech the memory operates by default at ddr5 4800 with timings lowered to 40 40 77 one Expo profile has been integrated by g-skill into the Trident Z5 Neo RGB the timings and dram voltage are locked at 30 30 8 38 96 and 1.35 volts respectively once the memory is activated and it switches to ddr56000 let's move on to the performance of the memory kit the ram kit performed admirably despite not being designed for Intel in terms of overall application performance the Trident Z5 Neo RGB came in second place just behind the Corsair Vengeance RGB ddr56000 C30 on the AMD x670 test system the Trident Z5 Neo RGB outperformed every other memory kit Additionally the Aram performed exceptionally well under productivity workloads such as Microsoft Office Adobe Photoshop Corona and handbrake although the gaming performance of each memory kit is very comparable the Trident Z5 Neo RGB came in second place overall as compared to the Corsair kit by just a bit the memory kit from g-skill features some of the tightest timings available as a result neither the trass nor the cast latency could shift however we had to increase the dram voltage to 1.4 volt in order to reduce the trcd and trp by two clock Cycles overclocking the ram kit to ddr5 6200 is a better technique to increase performance overall it's challenging to come up with arguments against the Trident Z5 Neo RGB ddr56000 C30 the memory kit provides reliable performance right out of the box and operates flawlessly that should convince the majority of customers all by themselves the Trident Z5 Neo rgb's quality exterior cannot be argued despite the fact that appearances are as always subjective g-skill employs SK hynix MDI ics for the memory kit the same as its Rivals there 4 memory modules still have some potential for customization hence this memory kit is highly recommended to those looking for an upgrade click on the video on your screen to compare it to the Corsair Vengeance memory kit in more detail than we did in this video
