Welcome to critical reviews today we're diving into the gskill Trident Z5 Neo a ram that promises high performance but might not deliver as expected let's explore the seven reasons you might want to think twice before purchasing this memory kit reason number seven compatibility issues while the Trident Z5 Neo claims to support both AMD Expo and Intel XMP many users have reported difficulties in getting it to work with their motherboards if your motherboard isn't on G skills qvl list you might be in for a headache reason number six pricey for what you get at around $189 you might expect topnotch performance however some competitors offer similar or even better specifications at a lower price point is the RGB lighting worth the extra bucks you might want to think again reason number five overclocking limitations while the Trident Z5 Neo is marketed for overclocking many users have found that achieving the advertised speeds requires a lot of tweaking and may not be stable if you're not an overclocking expert you might be better off with a more userfriendly option reason number four aesthetic over substance sure the RGB lighting looks great but if you're not into flashy Aesthetics you might find that it distracts from the overall performance sometimes less is more especially when it comes to Ram reason number three limited lifetime warranty while gskill offers a limited lifetime warranty it's not as reassuring as it sounds many users have reported long response times for warranty claims leaving you in Al Lurch if something goes wrong reason number two high heat output the Sleek aluminum heat spreaders look great but can get quite hot during heavy use if your system lacks proper cooling you might run into thermal throttling issues which can negate any performance gains and finally reason number one mixed reviews on performance despite the hype many users have reported that the performance doesn't live up to expectations especially in real world gaming scenarios you might find better options out there that won't leave you feeling disappointed so there you have it seven reasons to think twice about the g-skill Trident Z5 Neo remember while it might look good on paper the real world performance can tell a different story thanks for watching critical reviews and don't forget to like And subscribe for more honest tech reviews

Hey guys when it comes to Ryzen CPUs we are all  aware that memory plays a crucial role unlike with   Intel CPUs higher frequency isn't always better  in this case for the Ryzen 7000 and 9000 series   the sweet spot for DDR5 is 6,000 MHz in order  to maintain 1:1 ratio with the memory controller   so UCLk should be equal to MCLK since memory  manufacturers are well aware of this instead of   pushing higher frequency they've started focusing  on tighter timings for Ryzen optimized EXPO memory   kits so I went ahead and grabbed G.Skill latest  DDR5 6000 CL26 memory kit specifically the Trident   Z5 Royal Neo and it's their latest memory kit  for Ryzen with the lowest timing they've ever   offered and largely marketed for DDR5 but that's  not all i also picked up another kit from Lexar   Ares Gen 2 which also features CL26 so you see  both kits share the same main CAS latency but   as we'll see later on there are some notable  difference in sub-timing ones so if you are   looking for the best performance with the lowest  memory timings in this video I'll be comparing   both kits in various scenarios and benchmarks in  order to see which one truly earns the performance   crown here I am in the BIOS to show you first up  is the G.Skill Trident Z5 Royal Neo i've enabled   the EXPO profile it ships with and here we can  see the standard timings and the sub ones it has and you can see that I've installed actually  four sticks each one has 16GB for a total of   64GB running at EXPO and they have been  fully stable on my PC without any    modifications I've done next we have here  the Lexar ARES Gen 2 i also enabled here   EXPO and confirmed the profile correctly  applied and here are the timings they have it's the same capacity setup 4 16GB sticks  totaling 64GB now placing the two side by side   we can start to spot some difference while both  models use SK Hynix memory chips some timings   differ slightly the G.Skill Trident Z5 Royal Neo  runs at 26-36-36-96 where the Lexar ARES Gen 2   has 26-36-36-68 so you can see that Lexar offers  a lower tRAS in its primary timing but that's not   all also bank cycle time tRC is also different  132 clocks for G.Skill versus 104 for Lexar along   with a slightly lower tRDRDDD by 1 but still for  me personally the lower tRAS on the Lexar really   caught up my attention and that's why it actually  made me decide to compare them and ultimately make   this video however on the bright side Z5 Royal Neo  operates at a slightly lower voltage at 1.4V so it   might allow you to tweak it further to match  or even surpass Lexar timings for this video   though I'm testing both kits as they come out  out of the box using the EXPO profile for 100%   stability so during benchmarks the CPU I'll be  using is the Beast Ryzen 9950X3D with fully tuned   PBO and curve shaper and here are my current  curve values and are very stable you can check   out this suggested video here to learn more about  my PBO tuning for it as for the motherboard I'll   be using it's Aorus flagship AMD board the X870E  Xtreme AI top i got to say this board is amazing   in my opinion rock-solid stability excellent VRM a  massive heat sink for drives and all you need for   fans or ARGB headers and most importantly given  its lower price than competition like Asus Extreme   or MSI Godlike I would say it's a decent choice  now in order to avoid any GPU bottlenecking for   gaming benchmarks we'll see later on I'm pairing  the setup with the RTX 5090 Aorus Master it's   also a fantastic GPU i've done multiple videos on  it covering overclocking coil whine and testing   out the 4th fan it come with for extra cooling so  by using it I'm quite confident it's the perfect   fit for this memory testing now before  getting started with any benchmark and all testing   RAM stability is crucial even when we are using  an EXPO profile so I always recommend you test   it out on your system like make sure to do some  stress test for RAM etc whether by using this RAM   test software or by using AIDA64 system stability  which is trial free anyway I made sure both kits   were fully stable by running RAM stress test for  about an hour on each with four sticks both came   back with zero errors now the time for benchmark  starting with AIDA64 memory benchmark for read,   write, copy and latency now you can see here  are the results we've got for read speed G.Skill   Trident Z5 Royal Neo scored 81,467 MB/s while  Lexar ARES Gen 2 82,170 MB/s for write speed both   scored similarly slightly above 86,000 MB/s as  for copy speed again very close with Lexar being   ahead by about 200 MB/s but that could also  fall within margin of error finally latency is   identical on both at 68ns so as far as in this  synthetic test both kits perform nearly the same   now for fun I wanted to try on Cinebench 2024  even if it doesn't really benefit for lower   memory timings it's more of a CPU benchmark but I  tested it anyway on multicore the Lexar ARES Gen   2 scored just 7 points higher which is within  the margin of error so again performance here is   essentially the same but now here where things  get interesting as soon as we switch to gaming   test we'll start to notice some difference so  I tested 10 different games at 1080p on Low   settings to remove any GPU bottlenecking  and to better highlight CPU and memory behavior so   in Cyberpunk 2077 ARES Gen 2 came out ahead  by 6 FPS on average and 4 better in low FPS but next in Black Myth: Wukong G.Skill actually  performed better by around 3 FPS on average with   exact same low FPS so I don't know which one  could be better in this case i would say that   they're about the same like given that we have  the same low FPS maybe it could be around the same third game we have is Counter-Strike  2 here ARES gen 2 led by about 4 FPS   on average with exact same low FPS so it  could be around the same on G.Skill our   next game is Horizon Zero Dawn here we got  Royal Neo at 380 FPS on average while ARES   Gen 2 at 384 with higher low FPS so it  overall gave a slight edge to ARES Gen 2 while in Borderlands 3 no average FPS  difference but ARES Gen 2 had better   0.1% lows as you can see there's like less  FPS drop in Shadow of the Tomb Raider ARES   Gen 2 claimed 3 more FPS on average and  noticeably better low FPS by around 8 now in this one Dirt 5, ARES gen 2 showed a  jump of 6 FPS higher on average with low also   improved by 6 as well so you can see in some  titles we get higher gains with lower memory   timings even if they are different in two or  three types so not necessarily all of them next we have is Age of mythology retold while  both scored exactly the same a 1831.88 in the   game benchmark but our average FPS is actually  higher on ARES Gen 2 by 3 FPS now let's head into   a title with game engine that tends to benefit  from higher cache and better memory timings such   as Assassin's Creed Shadows here ARES Gen 2 had  a big lead a 192 versus 180 FPS average so we   got 12 higher averages with much much better lows  i got to say it's truly significant on this one now finally in Rainbow Six Siege no advantage for  ARES Gen 2 because Royal Neo scored somehow better one thing that made me confused though in this  kit Lexar claims latency as low as 55ns in   their marketing but in AIDA64 as you saw when we  tested it got actually 68ns so what's the deal?   well it turns out that that latency  they market is often measured under ideal lab   conditions not in a real world Windows environment  or perhaps with two sticks rather than four so   don't expect that 55ns number to show up in  your system it's more of a theoretical spec   for marketing anyway that wraps it up for this  video we saw that Lexar ARES gen 2 did offer   some improvement in some gaming titles given  its lower tRAS, tRC and tRDRDDD but nothing   massively game-changing both kits are incredibly  fast and feature ultra tight timings i believe   you could still tweak the G.Skill kit to reach  lower latency but I kept everything stock for   this test.So after seeing this if you had  to pick which one would you go for?Also let   me know in the comments which RAM kit you are  currently using for your Ryzen or even if it's   Intel there is no preference i like both equally  and I really hope that this video has helped you   understand the subtle difference when considering  low latency memory kits for your Ryzen build if   you liked this video please give it a thumbs  up and subscribe to the channel and check out   my other tuning and optimization guides thanks  for watching and I'll see you in the next one

Since the launch of Intel's Alder Lake processors, DDR5 has started its move into the hands of everyday consumers, and the Kingston FURY Beast DDR5-5200 2x16GB kit is a very compelling first entrant.In terms of physical design, it may lack any illumination but it keeps a low-profile look, both in dimensions and appearances.As always, it is backed by Kingston's lifetime warranty, similar to other memory manufacturers.In terms of performance, the Kingston FURY Beast is capable at keeping up with competition, despite its relatively slower transfer rates of 5200MT/s.Compared to other DDR5 kits we have tested, the FURY Beast definitely fell behind in the synthetic memory tests, but it was fine in the real-world simulation tests.It still provided better synthetic benchmark results compared to the departing DDR4 kits.When it came to overclocking, the Micron chips did have some extra wiggle room, allowing us to push the Kingston FURY Beast to DDR5-5600 with no relaxation of latency timings, which represents a small but notable 7.7% increase.This overclock was Prime95 stable, as shown in our screenshot above, and you can tighten up the CAS latency timings.All in all, the Kingston FURY Beast DDR5-5200 2x16GB is the first step for Kingston in their gaming DDR5 lineup, but I am quite pleased with the result we have here.At the time of the review, the Kingston FURY Beast DDR5-5200 2x16GB set can be found for $185, which is one of the most affordable DDR5 memory you can get.Obviously, this is a price increase compared to the out-going DDR4 options, but I think Kingston has priced their kits competitively without compromising on its capabilities to make this a performer.

64 GB of RGB RAM for $700.Kingston's betting you won't check the fine print before your motherboard starts memory training for the third consecutive minute.The Fury Beast KF560C30BK2-64 dominates Amazon's DDR5 listings with flashy lighting and AMD Expo certification.Two 32 gig sticks running at 6,000 mega transfers with CL30 timings.On paper, this hits the sweet spot for modern gaming.>> I
